title: Co-hosted technical webinar with AtlasFlow — logistics & assets
preview:
From: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@atlasflow.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 18 Feb 2027 15:03:00 -0800
Subject: Partnership — co-hosted webinar on inference scale patterns

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the intro at the partner forum last week — AtlasFlow is interested in running a joint technical webinar with Redwood to surface real customer patterns for scaling LLM inference (batching, caching, and routing). Our goals: drive developer signups, capture leads for joint POCs, and produce a short blog follow-up.

High-level idea: 45-minute technical demo + 15-minute live Q&A. We can bring 1-2 customers for short case snippets if that works. We tend to run these on Thursdays in APAC/EMEA-friendly windows but are flexible.

Would Redwood be interested? If so, can we lock a tentative April date and start an asset list (speaker names, slide decks, draft landing page)?

Attaching a rough agenda template so we can align.

Cheers,
Elena
Elena Ruiz
Head of Partnerships, AtlasFlow
elena.ruiz@atlasflow.io
(415) 555-0183

Attachment: AtlasFlow_DraftAgenda_20270414.pdf (PDF)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@atlasflow.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 19 Feb 2027 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Partnership — co-hosted webinar on inference scale patterns

Hi Elena — excited to run this with AtlasFlow.

Quick answers: yes, we’re interested; April works for us — tentative preference for the week of April 12–16. We’ll target a developer/engineering audience (SREs and ML infra). 60 mins total with a demo + panel Q&A sounds perfect.

Marissa (marketing) and Karthik (solutions) are cc’d to scope MKT and tech logistics. Next steps from our side:
- Marissa: confirm promotion plan / registration cadence
- Karthik: draft demo runbook and list of infra artifacts we’ll demo (KV cache, batching metrics, routing policy example)

Can you confirm 2–3 potential dates/times in that week and a preferred timezone? Also please send speaker bios and any customer names you’d want to include.

Thanks — Ben

Ben Carter
Head of Field Marketing, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

To: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@atlasflow.io>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 23 Feb 2027 11:09:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Partnership — co-hosted webinar on inference scale patterns

Hi Elena — Marissa from Redwood demand gen here. Glad to be onboard. A few logistical asks so we can get the event page and promo lined up: 

1) Confirmed date: April 14, 2027 at 09:00 PT (please confirm ASAP).
2) Speaker list: please send 1–2-line bios + headshots (png/jpg).
3) Registration host: we prefer AtlasFlow to host the landing page and share an embed/form we can co-brand (less admin friction).
4) Assets we need from AtlasFlow by March 18: speaker bios/headshots, logo (SVG), 1 pager for social, and any customer quotes/legal approvals.
5) Redwood assets: we’ll produce the event description, promotion copy, a shared social calendar, and an email cadence (T-14, T-7, T-1).
6) Recording and sequencing: we’ll handle post-demo edit; share raw recording if AtlasFlow hosts.

I’ve attached our webinar spec doc that outlines image sizes, template copy, and the promo timeline. Karthik — can you own the demo runbook and provide a short list of infra metrics we should capture in the session?

Elena — if you’re open, we’d like to do a short blog swap after the webinar: AtlasFlow writes a 700–900 word post on how they use routing policies; Redwood will write a short recap with operational lessons. We can cross-post the week after the replay goes live.

Thanks — Marissa

title: Outbound shielding & IP assignments for private endpoints
Post-call: latency acceptance checklist & tradeoffs
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@luminacare.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 25 Feb 2027 15:00:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call: latency acceptance checklist & tradeoffs

Emily — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from our side and proposed next steps so we have alignment before drafting a PoC scope:

Summary of what we heard
- Target acceptance: p95 and p99 end-to-end (client -> LuminaCare app -> Redwood hosted -> response) are the gating metrics. You emphasized p99 as the hard constraint for user-facing flows.
- Current baseline: you mentioned incumbent numbers during call (please confirm/attach traces).
- Traffic profile: steady-state ~300 QPS, peak bursts to ~1k QPS; streaming sessions (long-lived) expected concurrency ~40–60 during peak.
- Payload: average prompt ~650 tokens (post-tokenization), typical response ~420 tokens.

Redwood hosted rough feasibility notes
- Short answer: we can run a hosted PoC that will quantify end-to-end p95/p99 against a mirror of your traffic. Preliminary expectation (model/config dependent): p95 in the 120–200 ms range; p99 in the 300–700 ms range. We will provide a tighter projection once we see sample traces and the model variant you plan to use.
- Key tradeoffs: batching and continuous batching improve throughput and unit cost but increase tail latency; prefix/KV caching and eager-kv warm-up are the biggest lever to tighten p99 for repeated prefixes. Quantization reduces CPU/GPU time but can slightly shift tokenization latency.

Immediate asks / next steps
1) Emily/Jordan: please send sample request traces (headers + timing) and a couple of representative payloads. Attach as .json or a small gzipped trace.
2) LuminaCare: confirm the target acceptance numbers (exact p95/p99 thresholds) and whether streaming sessions are allowed to degrade to a higher p99 during spikes.
3) Ben: can you draft a short PoC test plan (traffic shaping, latency instrumentation, rollback criteria) and a latency matrix we can share with LuminaCare.
4) I'll propose dates for a two-week PoC window once we get traces (proposed week of Mar 8).

Attachments: latency_matrix_v1.xlsx (first-pass), poc-test-plan.md (outline)

Thanks — Avery
Avery Johnson | Account Executive, Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0187
Redwood Inference


From: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@luminacare.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Miles <jordan.miles@luminacare.com>
Date: Thu, 25 Feb 2027 17:14:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: latency acceptance checklist & tradeoffs

Avery — thanks for the clear recap. A few clarifications from our side so your PoC plan can be targeted:

- Confirmed acceptance thresholds: p95 <= 180 ms, p99 <= 500 ms for our primary customer-facing inference path. If p99 occasionally spikes during large-system events, we can tolerate short windows up to 700 ms but the goal is 500 ms.
- Traffic: steady 300 QPS average, sustained 15-minute bursts up to 1k QPS during business hours. Streaming concurrency: typically 45 active sessions; if you need a safety cap, design for 60 concurrent streaming sessions.
- Current provider baseline (for reference): p95 ~270 ms; p99 ~1.2 s. We will attach sample traces (sanitized) in a follow-up email.

Two asks before PoC schedule:
1) Can you include a short section in the PoC plan describing how Redwood measures end-to-end (we need to correlate our client timestamps with your server-side timestamps) and how you instrument p99.
2) Do you have a recommended approach for streaming chunk size / client-side windowing to minimize tail latency?

We can aim for the week of Mar 8 for the PoC. I’ll send the traces by EOD tomorrow.

— Emily
Emily Zhao | Head of Platform Engineering, LuminaCare
emily.zhao@luminacare.com | +1 212-555-0144


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@luminacare.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 26 Feb 2027 08:42:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: latency acceptance checklist & tradeoffs

Emily — looping in technical details and the PoC scaffolding. Long reply but I wanted to capture the measurement approach and the tradeoffs explicitly.

Measurement & instrumentation
- End-to-end definition: measure from the client HTTP request start to the final token delivered to the client over the same connection. For streaming flows include time-to-first-byte and time-to-last-byte. We'll provide an SDK snippet to emit client-side timestamps that we can correlate with our server traces (trace id propagation).
- p95/p99 computation: we'll compute with a sliding-window 1-minute granularity and report both raw percentiles and 95/99 computed from fully-aggregated logs to match your analytics.

Expected performance envelope (hosted PoC, initial config)
- Baseline assumptions: using our optimized serving runtime, with model variant X-small quantized FP16 on A100-class GPUs, and warmed KV cache for repeated prefixes. Incoming prompt/response sizes as you described.
- Expected ranges (examples — will tighten after traces): p95: 120–180 ms; p99: 320–520 ms. If traffic is heavily bursty and prefix cache miss rate is high, p99 can move toward 700+ ms.

Key levers & tradeoffs
1) Batching: increases throughput and lowers cost per token. Strong batching (longer batching windows) will push tail up (add 20–80 ms depending on queueing). For your acceptance, we recommend a conservative batching timeout (<= 5 ms) during the PoC to prioritize p99.
2) Prefix/KV caching: for repeated prompts this is the most effective lever to reduce p99. We'll instrument cache hit rates and show the delta.
3) Streaming chunking: recommend 256-token chunks server-side and client-side windowing of 150–300 ms to smooth spikes without penalizing time-to-first-byte. We can provide a sample client implementation.
4) Autoscale/burst routing: we suggest a two-tier approach — reserved pool for steady 300 QPS and a burst pool + cross-region fallback to absorb 1k QPS spikes. Cross-region adds network RJM but helps avoid queuing.

PoC test plan highlights (attached as poc-test-plan.md)
- Traffic mirror vs synthetic replay: we prefer mirror for fidelity; if you can’t mirror, a high-fidelity replay of real traces is acceptable.
- Tests: baseline (low load), ramp (to 300 QPS sustained), burst test (1k QPS 15-min window), streaming concurrency ramp to 60.
- Acceptance criteria: p95 <= 180 ms AND p99 <= 500 ms for sustained and ramp tests; documented deviation windows allowed for <5% of test duration.
- Rollback/fail: automatic cutover rules and a fallback to your incumbent in case p99 exceeds 1s for more than 10% of test duration.

Attachments included: latency_matrix_v1.xlsx (metric buckets and target cells), poc-test-plan.md (test steps + instrumentation). Drive link: https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/1A2b_latency_poc

We’ll prepare the staging endpoints and a short SDK snippet for timestamp propagation tomorrow.

Ben Carter
Senior Performance Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

text:
From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Emily Zhao <emily.zhao@luminacare.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 25 Feb 2027 15:00:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call: latency acceptance checklist & tradeoffs

Emily — thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from our side and proposed next steps so we have alignment before drafting a PoC scope:

Summary of what we heard
- Target acceptance: p95 and p99 end-to-end (client -> LuminaCare app -> Redwood hosted -> response) are the gating metrics. You emphasized p99 as the hard constraint for user-facing flows.
- Current baseline: you mentioned incumbent numbers during call (please confirm/attach traces).
- Traffic profile: steady-state ~300 QPS, peak bursts to ~1k QPS; streaming sessions (long-lived) expected concurrency ~40–60 during peak.
- Payload: average prompt ~650 tokens (post-tokenization), typical response ~420 tokens.

Redwood hosted rough feasibility notes
- Short answer: we can run a hosted PoC that will quantify end-to-end p95/p99 against a mirror of your traffic. Preliminary expectation (model/config dependent): p95 in the 120–200 ms range; p99 in the 300–700 ms range. We will provide a tighter projection once we see sample traces and the model variant you plan to use.
- Key tradeoffs: batching and continuous batching improve throughput and unit cost but increase tail latency; prefix/KV caching and eager-kv warm-up are the biggest lever to tighten p99 for repeated prefixes. Quantization reduces CPU/GPU time but can slightly shift tokenization latency.

Immediate asks / next steps
1) Emily/Jordan: please send sample request traces (headers + timing) and a couple of representative payloads. Attach as .json or a small gzipped trace.
2) LuminaCare: confirm the target acceptance numbers (exact p95/p99 thresholds) and whether streaming sessions are allowed to degrade to a higher p99 during spikes.
3) Ben: can you draft a short PoC test plan (traffic shaping, latency instrumentation, rollback criteria) and a latency matrix we can share with LuminaCare.
4) I'll propose dates for a two-week PoC window once we get traces (proposed week of Mar 8).

Attachments: latency_matrix_v1.xlsx (first-pass), poc-test-plan.md (outline)

title: Host tenancy mapping + data-flow annotated deliverables (SOC2/SIG request)
preview:
From: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@bigbank.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 20 Feb 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Host tenancy mapping + data-flow artifacts for SOC2/SIG review

Camila,

Thanks for taking this — BigBank is in the final stages of their vendor risk review and the SOC2/SIG team raised a few follow-ups specific to Dedicated and Private deployments. Their ask is twofold: an annotated "host tenancy mapping" showing which compute pools are used by Dedicated vs Private customers and a data-flow diagram that highlights encryption-in-transit boundaries, KMS boundaries, and any cross-tenant egress paths.

Concretely they requested:
- A single-page annotated network diagram (PDF/SVG) showing control plane vs data plane, edge routing, and optional on-prem connectors.
- A short mapping table (CSV) listing components, tenancy model (shared/isolated/dedicated host), and any customer-facing network endpoints.
- A one-paragraph narrative describing fallback behaviour (e.g., model fallback routes, cross-region failover) and any observable telemetry we emit (logs/flow logs).

Can you confirm turnaround? Ideally by Tue EOD so we can attach to the SIG questionnaire.

Oliver
Security Engineer — BigBank Financial Services
security@bigbank.com



From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@bigbank.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, 20 Feb 2027 11:35:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Host tenancy mapping + data-flow artifacts for SOC2/SIG review

Hi Oliver — appreciate the clear list. We can produce the requested artifacts. Outline below and next steps: 

Planned deliverables (target: Tue 2027-02-22 EOD):
- redwood_host_tenancy_mapping_v1.pdf (single-page annotated diagram) — shows Dedicated GPU pools, Private VPC connectors, control plane endpoints, and optional air-gapped flows.
- redwood_network_annotated.svg — vector version for your architecture team to inspect; will include layer callouts.
- service_boundary_matrix.csv — component / tenancy / encryption / KMS column mapping.
- Short narrative (one paragraph) included in the PDF and in the email body; call out fallback/routing behaviour.

A few clarifications I need before we finalize:
1) For "Private" deployments are you specifically referencing customer-managed on-prem packages (air-gapped) or VPC-hosted Private with our control plane? We will flag differences in the diagram.
2) Do you need any packet-capture evidence or flow logs, or is the mapping sufficient for SIG review?

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Oliver Grant <oliver.grant@bigbank.com>
Date: Mon, 22 Feb 2027 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Host tenancy mapping + data-flow artifacts for SOC2/SIG review

Camila / Oliver — draft artifacts attached. Quick technical summary here so the reviewers have context: 

Attachment notes: redwood_network_annotated.svg (vector) and service_boundary_matrix.csv.

Technical highlights (short):
- Dedicated: customers on Dedicated are scheduled onto reserved GPU pools (node-pinning optional) within our tenant-fenced Kubernetes clusters. Network isolation is achieved via VPC per-tenant routing + host-network policies; data-plane egress is limited to per-tenant NAT gateways where required. All inter-node traffic is encrypted using mTLS; KV caches and model weights rest on encrypted block devices (AES-256).
- Private (VPC-hosted): control plane calls (telemetry, billing, model catalog) can be routed over a peered, TLS-protected control plane endpoint. Customer-managed Private (air-gapped) has an explicit "sync appliance" flow documented in the SVG — no outbound connections from compute nodes by default.
- KMS: Customer-managed Private options can either (a) use Redwood KMS via a peered HSM interface, or (b) integrate their own KMS/HSM; column in CSV shows kms_integration: redwood_kms | customer_kms.
- Fallbacks: when a Dedicated variant is unavailable, the request is routed to a compatible hosted variant in a lower-cost pool; routing decisions logged to the request trace and anonymized in metrics.

Drive link (preview): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aB2cExampleLink/redwood-evidence

Attachment stubs included: redwood_network_annotated.svg, service_boundary_matrix.csv, redwood_host_tenancy_mapping_v1.pdf

Hi Avery,

Quick one — we noticed a significant usage spike on Feb 12–13 tied to our staging migration (we routed a few batch tests to prod accidentally). Our billing dashboard shows ~2.3M extra tokens and an associated one-off charge that will push us a material amount over our committed capacity for Q1.

Can Redwood help reconcile this and suggest options for a one-time adjustment or credit? We can pull the raw run logs but wanted to get a plan in motion while we gather them. Purchasing is looped to track any formal PO/invoice steps.

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Sofia Ramos <sofia.ramos@alcove.com>
Cc: avery_johnson@redwood.com, laura_bennett@redwood.com, purchasing@alcove.com
Date: Fri, 19 Feb 2027 08:30:00 -0800
Subject: Proposed settlement: Alcove Q1 staging spike

Sofia —

Thanks for the export, and for the clear notes on root cause. Finance reviewed Alcove’s usage and our proposed approach is below (aiming to be fair while respecting committed capacity).

Proposal (one-time):
- Gross overage charge calculated: $12,450 (covers the 2.3M token overage at your plan rates).
- Goodwill credit applied: $4,500 (we can apply this immediately as a credit toward the overage).
- Net one-time top-up due: $7,950.
- Operational concession: 90 days of free burst capacity (up to 500k tokens/month) to help with testing during your migration window.

We’ll issue an adjusted invoice (ALC-3342) for the net amount. If you prefer, we can apply the net as a credit against your next renewal invoice; otherwise we’ll generate a short-form invoice payable under your standard PO terms.

I attached a simple schedule showing the math and suggested invoice wording. If you want a smaller immediate payment and the rest amortized into the renewal, we can model that as well.

Let us know which path Alcove prefers and we’ll route the DocuSign or updated invoice.

— Ben Carter
Billing Lead, Redwood Inference
(Attachment: proposed_credit_schedule.pdf)

title: URGENT: ScaleCart production failures after gamma release — immediate rollback request
preview:
From: Jamie Park <jamie@scalecart.com>
To: ava.chen@redwood.ai
Cc: sre@scalecart.com
Date: 2027-02-20T08:12:00-08:00
Subject: Production: spike in 5xx/429 immediately after gamma-v2-quant rollout

Ava —

We just pushed your gamma-v2-quant variant at 07:45 UTC for our product recommendations endpoint (canary -> full rollout per instructions). Within ~10–30 minutes we saw an error amplification: 5xx rate jumped from ~2.4% to 12.7% and 429s from ~0.1% to 9.3%. Latency tails spiked and clients started retrying aggressively, which made the issue worse.

We're cutting a handful of numbers from our monitoring:
- Peak 5xx window (07:58–08:05 UTC): 15%
- 429 count increased 90x relative to baseline in 20m
- P95 latency went from 180ms -> 1.1s

Attached are a trimmed packet of logs and a chart (support_logs_20270220.zip, error_rate_surge_chart.png). Our SRE (cc) is standing by. Please advise next steps — we need immediate rollback or a protected fallback. This is impacting checkout conversion for several merchants.

We need an executive-level contact; this is escalated in our ops channel.

Thanks,
Jamie Park
Head of Engineering, ScaleCart
---



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Cc: jamie@scalecart.com, sre@scalecart.com
Date: 2027-02-20T08:20:00-08:00
Subject: Fwd: Production: spike in 5xx/429 immediately after gamma-v2-quant rollout — immediate assistance required

Team — forwarding ScaleCart's report. They rolled gamma-v2-quant at ~07:45 UTC and are seeing an error / retry amplification. They requested rollback if we can't stabilize quickly.

Quick asks:
- Hannah: can we enforce a routing fallback (pin their traffic to gamma-v1 variant) at the edge and add per-customer throttling?
- Rishi/Jonas: can you validate the model runtime/quantization kernel selection and look for regressions (memory/latency anomalies) tied to the v2 release?
- Marissa: please prep an exec note (1–2 bullets) I can send to VP of Sales if ScaleCart asks for exec escalation.

I've attached the logs they sent and a couple of our dashboards. I am starting a 30 minute war-room.

Attachments: support_logs_20270220.zip, error_rate_surge_chart.png

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Account Executive — Enterprise (Redwood Inference)


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
Cc: jamie@scalecart.com
Date: 2027-02-20T08:34:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Production: spike in 5xx/429 — initial runtime triage (gamma-v2-quant)

Short summary of what I can see from the traces + our internal telemetry (INF-7241):
- We enabled an FP8-optimized kernel path for gamma-v2-quant that should have been gated for sequence lengths <= 256. ScaleCart traffic has bursts of long sequences (avg 420 tokens) which appears to trigger an untested code-path.
- Symptoms: per-worker KV-cache warm-up + kernel mismatch leads to increased memory pressure and occasional RPC failures (502s) and local queue backpressure -> 429s at the gateway.
- Evidence: 
  * kernel selection logs show fallback oscillation between fp8-opt and fallback-fp16 on the same requests (see runtime_kv_dump.txt attached).
  * OOM/eviction spikes aligned with the error window in region us-west-2.

Immediate mitigations I recommend (in order):
1) Roll gamma traffic back to gamma-v1 (float path) for ScaleCart only — this is fastest.
2) Disable the new fp8 kernel auto-selection flag (hotfix rollback) and redeploy runtime binary to canary before re-enabling.
3) As a protective measure, temporarily lower max_batch_size and disable cross-request batching for this tenant to reduce memory compaction pressure.

I'll open a dedicated infra/serving patch (INF-7241) and attach a minimal hotfix PR with the selection flag inverted. If someone can coordinate the rollback with edge routing (Hannah), I will prepare the binary rollback.

Rishi
Lead, Applied ML Systems — Redwood Inference

title: Zip SSO invite blocked by device clock skew
preview:
From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@sundale.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 20 Feb 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Supplier portal invite - acceptance failing

Hi Aditya,

We tried to accept the Zip supplier invite you sent yesterday but the page returns an error immediately after clicking the accept button: "Invite token invalid (clock skew)". It looks like an auth check is rejecting the token on our side.

I attached the client-side trace (trace-log.txt) and a quick screenshot. Our IT team confirmed the user's phone was a corporate-managed Android (v13) with automatic time enabled — I can double-check the device clock if that helps.

Can you resend or provide an alternate link? We need to finish onboarding so we can receive the first PO from Redwood next week.

Thanks,
Liam
Liam O'Connor
IT Operations, Sundale Manufacturing
liam.oconnor@sundale.com
+1 415-555-0192

Attachment: trace-log.txt (text)

From: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
To: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@sundale.com>
Cc: 
Date: Sat, 20 Feb 2027 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Supplier portal invite - acceptance failing

Thanks for the debug log, Liam — sorry for the friction here.

Priyom / Ben — can you take a look? Vendor reports an "clock skew" error during Zip SSO acceptance. Trace suggests the client presents an iat/exp outside the tolerance window. Quick ideas:
- Is Zip configured with a strict 5m validation window for the invite token?
- Can we issue a one-off invite that extends TTL to 30m or uses a different HMAC seed?
- If not, can we provide a direct enrollment link (no SSO) as a temporary bypass?

Liam — we can try a resend with a longer expiration while we investigate. If you can share the exact timestamp from the device (or confirm NTP status) that would help pinpoint whether it's device-side drift or a token TTL issue.

Attaching the current invite stub (invite_link.html) for reference.

-Aditya

Attachment: invite_link.html (HTML stub)

From: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 20 Feb 2027 13:27:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: trace-log.txt / analysis

Forwarding the relevant bits from our infra colleague (cut for brevity) — quote from the auth service trace: 

"[2027-02-20T16:09:12Z] auth.token.validate: token.exp=1677001752 token.iat=1677001452 now=1677001212 skew=240
error=EXP_BEFORE_ISSUE, signature_ok=true"

Interpretation: the vendor's client clock appears ~240s ahead of our server 'now' value which pushes the token verification into the fail path under the current 5-minute allowed skew. Zip's SSO library enforces iat/exp with default +/- 300s, but our invite generation sets a conservative 5-minute TTL and rejects tokens where iat > now + 30s (defense against replay). That combination can produce this exact rejection when NTP hasn't synchronized.

Workarounds:
- Short term: resend invite with TTL bumped to 30m and relax the iat future tolerance to 2 minutes for invite paths only (INF-8921).
- Medium term: add client-guidance to the invite email that instructs users to enable automatic time or check device settings; add a pre-check on accept that surfaces a friendly error with steps.
- Long term: make invite acceptance tolerant to reasonable drift by comparing server time to STS or using monotonic counters.

I'll file INF-8921 and propose the one-off TTL bump. Ben, can you issue the extended invite and confirm link generation parameters?

— Priyom

Related: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/INF-8921

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@sundale.com>
Date: Sun, 21 Feb 2027 09:00:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Supplier portal invite - extended TTL sent

Hi all,

I generated a new Zip invite and extended the acceptance TTL to 30 minutes. I also toggled the invite validation path to allow a 2-minute future-iat tolerance for invite acceptance only — this is a scoped, temporary change that will be rolled back after the vendor completes onboarding. The new link is attached (invite_link_extended.html) and I included acceptance steps in the email body below.

Liam — steps for Sundale:
1) Click the attached link from the device you will use to accept (mobile or desktop).
2) If you still see "clock skew" errors, please check device time settings and toggle "Automatic date & time" off then on again, restart the browser and retry.
3) If acceptance succeeds, reply here and we'll mark the account active and push the first PO.

If the issue persists, we'll temporarily issue a non-SSO enrollment code and complete a manual verification.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Operations Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

Attachment: invite_link_extended.html (HTML stub)

From: Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@sundale.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 21 Feb 2027 11:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Supplier portal invite - extended TTL sent

Ben / Aditya,

That worked — the extended link allowed the user to accept and complete the profile. Our desktop had an industry VPN that was rewriting NTP packets; once we tested on a non-VPN device acceptance completed immediately.

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the clear steps. Please keep INF-8921 open if you plan a permanent fix; we'll test again if you roll the change back.

Best,
Liam O'Connor
IT Operations, Sundale Manufacturing
liam.oconnor@sundale.com
+1 415-555-0192

title: Preferred vendor form + PO mapping for commit tiers
preview:
From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>; Procurement <procurement@acme.com>
Date: 2027-02-20T09:12:00Z
Subject: Request: preferred vendor form + commit tiers clarification

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week — the team liked the latencies and the cost transparency. Procurement ran the preferred-vendor form you sent and flagged two issues we need clarified before we can process a PO:

1) The form asks for an annual commit and then lists per-token tiered pricing (Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3). Our finance system needs a clear mapping from those tiers to PO line items (we can't process a line that says \"variable usage\").

2) We typically require either (a) a fixed monthly invoice amount tied to an annual commitment, or (b) a usage-based PO where overages are billed per unit but must have a hard cap or a pre-approved escalation path. The vendor form as-is doesn't match either pattern.

Could you provide: (i) a sample PO mapping (two or three line-item examples) that reflect your commit tiers; (ii) a one-paragraph explanation of how overages are handled and whether we can cap spend for the first quarter?

I've attached the preferred vendor PDF our legal/procurement team completed for your records. Also looping in Priya from Acme legal.

Appreciate a quick turnaround — procurement hopes to issue a PO by Friday if this is clarified.

Thanks,
Sanjay Rao
Procurement Manager
Acme Corp

Attachment: Acme_PreferredVendor_Form.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@acme.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-02-20T10:03:00Z
Subject: RE: Request: preferred vendor form + commit tiers clarification

Hi Sanjay — thanks for the clear note and the timeline.

Quick answers up front: we can support both billing patterns you described. Attached is a short spreadsheet (Redwood_PO_Mapping_Sample.xlsx) that shows 3 common PO mappings we use:

- Option A: Annual committed $ (invoiced monthly) — PO line = \"Redwood inference committed spend\" with monthly invoice amount.
- Option B: Usage-based with committed baseline — PO line 1 = \"Committed tokens (annualized)\" at discounted rate; PO line 2 = \"Overage tokens\" at the published overage rate, with an explicit quarterly cap field.
- Option C: Notional commit — single PO line with annual value and internal memo mapping to tier buckets; useful for complicated procurement systems.

Overages: by default, overage is billed at the Tier N rate. For the first quarter, we can agree to a hard cap (e.g., 20% above committed tokens) and require manual approval to exceed that. If you'd prefer automatic throttling instead of billing, we can enable a soft-cap that blocks requests once the threshold is hit.

I'll attach a short pricing offer (Redwood_Pricing_Offer_v2.pdf) that lists the per-token tiers and the committed-tier discounts we propose for AC-2027-0045. If you send a draft PO, I can map our rates to your line items and return a redlined version to speed legal.

Would you be available for a 20-minute call at 11:30 PT to walk procurement/legal through the sample PO?

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Accounts — Redwood Inference

Attachments: Redwood_PO_Mapping_Sample.xlsx, Redwood_Pricing_Offer_v2.pdf


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-02-20T11:15:00Z
Subject: RE: Request: preferred vendor form + commit tiers clarification

Ben — looping in a couple quick guardrails from Sales Ops / Discounts:

- Standard approval: Sales reps can approve up to 10% off list pricing for a committed annual spend under $250k.
- For discounts between 10–20% or contract language changes (billing cadence, special caps) we need Sales Ops sign-off. Anything >20% requires director approval.
- Onboarding credit: we can offer a one-time onboarding credit (up to $15k) for deals that meet our ROI criteria; please include a short justification note.

Please confirm what you're proposing for Acme (percent off list, proposed onboarding credit, and preferred billing cadence). If you're asking for a special PO schema exception (e.g., fixed monthly plus dynamic overage with a manual approval step), call it out explicitly so I can route to Legal.

Also note — if procurement wants a hard-capped overage for Q1 we can accept that as a temporary condition without impacting long-term pricing tiers.

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Sales Operations Manager



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@acme.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>, Grace Oconnor <grace@redwood.ai>, Priya Shah <priya.shah@acme.com>
Date: 2027-02-20T15:27:00Z
Subject: RE: Request: preferred vendor form + commit tiers clarification

Sanjay — follow-up with the mapping and final asks:

Summary of what Redwood will accept for Acme (proposed, subject to signature):

1) PO structure (two-line example you can paste into your system):
   - Line 1: \"Redwood committed inference spend (annualized)\" — Quantity: 1; Unit price: $120,000; Unit description: Annual committed amount covering up to 40B tokens at Tier 1 rate.
   - Line 2: \"Redwood overage tokens\" — Unit price: $0.000012 per token; billed monthly for usage above the committed baseline; quarterly hard cap = 20% of committed tokens unless pre-approved in writing.

2) Discount/onboarding: 12% off published list for year 1, plus a $10,000 one-time onboarding credit applied to the first invoice. (Lauren: this is within the Sales Ops 10–20% band; requesting your sign-off.)

3) Billing & terms: Net-30 invoicing, monthly consumption reports, and itemized invoices mapping to the two PO line items. Legal has reviewed the standard SOW and is OK with the proposed billing cadence; acceptance contingent on receiving the PO that follows this mapping.

Next steps: If that sounds OK, please have procurement issue a PO using the two-line format above and attach the filled preferred-vendor form. Once we receive the PO we will countersign and issue the first monthly invoice with the onboarding credit applied.

Ben Carter

To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>; Procurement <procurement@acme.com>
Date: 2027-02-20T09:12:00Z
Subject: Request: preferred vendor form + commit tiers clarification

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week — the team liked the latencies and the cost transparency. Procurement ran the preferred-vendor form you sent and flagged two issues we need clarified before we can process a PO:

1) The form asks for an annual commit and then lists per-token tiered pricing (Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3). Our finance system needs a clear mapping from those tiers to PO line items (we can't process a line that says \"variable usage\").

2) We typically require either (a) a fixed monthly invoice amount tied to an annual commitment, or (b) a usage-based PO where overages are billed per unit but must have a hard cap or a pre-approved escalation path. The vendor form as-is doesn't match either pattern.

Could you provide: (i) a sample PO mapping (two or three line-item examples) that reflect your commit tiers; (ii) a one-paragraph explanation of how overages are handled and whether we can cap spend for the first quarter?

I've attached the preferred vendor PDF our legal/procurement team completed for your records. Also looping in Priya from Acme legal.

Appreciate a quick turnaround — procurement hopes to issue a PO by Friday if this is clarified.

Thanks,
Sanjay Rao
Procurement Manager
Acme Corp

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>

Date: 2027-02-20T10:03:00Z
Subject: RE: Request: preferred vendor form + commit tiers clarification

Hi Sanjay — thanks for the clear note and the timeline.

Quick answers up front: we can support both billing patterns you described. Attached is a short spreadsheet (Redwood_PO_Mapping_Sample.xlsx) that shows 3 common PO mappings we use:

- Option A: Annual committed $ (invoiced monthly) — PO line = \"Redwood inference committed spend\" with monthly invoice amount.
- Option B: Usage-based with committed baseline — PO line 1 = \"Committed tokens (annualized)\" at discounted rate; PO line 2 = \"Overage tokens\" at the published overage rate, with an explicit quarterly cap field.
- Option C: Notional commit — single PO line with annual value and internal memo mapping to tier buckets; useful for complicated procurement systems.

Overages: by default, overage is billed at the Tier N rate. For the first quarter, we can agree to a hard cap (e.g., 20% above committed tokens) and require manual approval to exceed that. If you'd prefer automatic throttling instead of billing, we can enable a soft-cap that blocks requests once the threshold is hit.

I'll attach a short pricing offer (Redwood_Pricing_Offer_v2.pdf) that lists the per-token tiers and the committed-tier discounts we propose for AC-2027-0045. If you send a draft PO, I can map our rates to your line items and return a redlined version to speed legal.

title: Request: Contract asks — termination rights & fee-withholding for ClearBox
preview:
From: Alexei Popov <alexei.popov@clearboxpay.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya@clearboxpay.com>, contracts@clearboxpay.com
Date: Sun, 14 Feb 2027 10:01:12 -0800
Subject: Request: Contract language for termination and fee-withholding (ClearBox account)

Hi Avery —

Thanks again for the demo last week. We're excited about the latency profile and reliability you showed, but our legal & compliance teams are pushing for stronger commercial remedies than standard service credits. Specifically they're asking for:

- A fee-withholding / escrow equivalent to 6 months of anticipated fees (to be released on successful operational milestones),
- Clear termination rights for repeat P1/P2 outages (we're talking sustained availability/lost throughput for payments), and
- A liquidated damages clause for direct processing losses tied to repeat SLA failures.

I attached a redline that our GC circulated; it's heavy-handed, but it outlines what they'd accept. Can your team review and tell us if any of this is workable and what alternatives Redwood would accept (capped escrow, staged buyout, cure periods, etc.)?

We need an initial position by EOD Thursday if possible — procurement is trying to line up roadmap approvals. I'd prefer a short call to walk through specifics.

Attachments: CB_SLA_Amendment_Redline.docx (redline from ClearBox GC)

Thanks,
Alexei Popov
Head of Payments Partnerships
ClearBox Payments


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Alexei Popov <alexei.popov@clearboxpay.com>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya@clearboxpay.com>
Date: Sun, 14 Feb 2027 11:06:03 -0800
Subject: Re: Request: Contract language for termination and fee-withholding (ClearBox account)

Alexei — thanks for the clear summary and the redline. I appreciate the urgency.

Quick note: we won't be able to accept an open-ended liquidated damages exposure as written, but we should find a commercially workable compromise. I'm looping in our legal & AM partners to give you a concrete proposal (capped escrow / staged buyout / enhanced ops guarantees). Can we set a 30-minute call on Wednesday (Feb 17) or Thursday morning?

I'll follow up with an internal POV and a proposed doc we can use as a starting point.

Best,
Avery Johnson
Account Executive — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 15 Feb 2027 09:22:10 -0800
Subject: Fwd: ClearBox contract asks — need legal + AM POV

Folks — forwarding ClearBox's redline (attached). Short summary: customer wants 6mo escrow, termination rights for repeat P1/P2, and liquidated damages for losses. Procurement is pushing for a position this week.

Questions for legal / ops / AM:
- Legal: how far can we go without creating open-ended monetary liability? Any precedent for escrowed fee-withholdings?
- AM: what's the account impact if we propose staged buyout vs hard escrow? Revenue / churn risk?
- Ops: can we offer an elevated monitoring + weekly ops review and commit to defined remediation windows?

Proposed commercial anchor (my initial thought): 3 months MRR escrow with staged release on milestones, termination only after 3 distinct severity-1 incidents impacting >=48 hours total in a 90-day rolling window, and a capped buyout equal to remaining 6 months at NRR. But this is negotiable — want legal input.

Attachments: CB_SLA_Amendment_Redline.docx, redline_suggestions_avery_v1.pdf

Thanks — Avery


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 15 Feb 2027 12:43:31 -0800
Subject: Re: Fwd: ClearBox contract asks — need legal + AM POV

Avery / team —

High-level legal position: we cannot accept uncapped liquidated damages or unconstrained escrow that is effectively a security for all customer risk. That creates systemic liability and insurance/regulatory complications. However, there are reasonable mitigations we can offer: a capped escrow/holdback equal to up to 3 months of committed fees (not 6), with explicit milestone release criteria; narrowly-tailored termination right that requires repeated, documented, severe SLA breaches with cure windows (e.g., three P1-equivalent incidents totaling >48 hours of degraded availability in 90 days) plus 30-day cure; and a clearly-capped buyout formula (e.g., remaining committed fees capped at 6x monthly average or a fixed multiplier).

We should avoid phrases like "liquidated damages for direct losses" unless we can tightly define loss categories and caps. Instead, propose structured cash holdback + enhanced credits + a one-time buyout option for repeated, unremediated failures. I'll attach a legal counter-redline with sample language and suggested caps.

Attachments: legal_counter_redline.docx

If we need to escalate commercially, Rafael can discuss exceptions with Sales Leadership, but legal prefers caps + structured cure as the starting point.

—Marissa
Deputy General Counsel
Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alexei Popov <alexei.popov@clearboxpay.com>, Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>, Maya Singh <maya@clearboxpay.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 18 Feb 2027 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Proposal: Compromise on ClearBox SLA remedies

Alexei / Maya —

Thanks for the patience while we reviewed your request. We value ClearBox as a strategic partner and want to get to terms that protect you without exposing Redwood to unlimited liability. Here's a concrete compromise we can present: 

1) Holdback / Escrow: 3 months of estimated billed fees held in escrow. Release schedule: 50% after month 3 if no P1 incidents in the period, remaining 50% after month 6 subject to milestone checks.

2) Remediation & Termination: Termination right only after three documented P1-equivalent incidents totalling >48 hours of degraded availability in any 90-day rolling window, each incident subject to a 30-day cure window.

3) Buyout / Cash Cap: If termination is triggered, a buyout option is available capped at 6 months of average spend (not uncapped LD).

4) Credits & Ops: Enhanced SLA credits (higher credit %) for qualifying incidents, plus weekly ops review and a dedicated escalation path with a named SRE lead while the deal is under escrow.

We can share a redline that adopts Marissa's suggested language for the holdback and cure timetable. If that direction sounds okay, I'll circulate the redline and a DocuSign packet for an amendment and SOW update.

DocuSign: https://docusign.net/clearbox-sla-v1
Attachment: SLA_proposal_ClearBox_20270218.pdf

Are you available for a 30-minute call Thurs 2/18 1:00–1:30pm PT to align?

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Manager
Redwood Inference


From: Alexei Popov <alexei.popov@clearboxpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.ai>
Cc: Maya Singh <maya@clearboxpay.com>, contracts@clearboxpay.com
Date: Thu, 18 Feb 2027 15:23:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Proposal: Compromise on ClearBox SLA remedies

Ben / Avery —

Thanks — that framing is helpful and much closer to what procurement will accept. The 3-month holdback + milestone release + capped buyout looks reasonable. Please send the redline and DocuSign, and let's take the 1:00pm PT slot today. I'll have our GC on the line for the call.

Attachments requested: the legal counter-redline you mentioned and the updated SOW.

Talk shortly —
Alexei

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Date: Sun, 14 Feb 2027 09:15:00 -0800
Subject: Borealis — technical session planning and calendar options

Hi Elena —

Great to connect earlier. Quick recap from our discovery call and a proposed path to get you into a hands-on technical session with the ML/infra team. We heard that Borealis' immediate priorities are: reducing per-request latency during peak inference, clarifying data isolation for PII, and validating throughput at 5–10 TPS for your 2k token average requests.

Proposed next step: a 60–90 minute technical session (engineers + infra + security) to:
- review current workload profile and sequence-length distribution
- validate model/quantization options and expected unit cost
- run a short capacity/latency Q&A and discuss fallback routing

Suggested windows (all PT):
- Tue 16 Feb, 10:00–11:15 (75m)
- Wed 17 Feb, 08:00–09:15 (75m)
- Thu 18 Feb, 13:00–14:30 (90m)

Please let me know which of these work or propose two alternatives. Also confirm attendees from your side (we recommend an ML lead, infra/ops, and a security/compliance contact).

Attaching a short discovery-notes.pdf and a one-page pre-read that outlines what the session will include.

Thanks — looking forward to locking this in.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena@brightleaf.ai>
Cc: Tom Gallagher <tgallagher@brightleaf.ai>
Date: Mon, 15 Feb 2027 13:12:00 -0500
Subject: Aligning demo time windows for BrightLeaf (US/EU/APAC)

Hi Elena / Tom —

Thanks again for the quick discovery call last week. I’m handing this over to Ava (our EMEA AE) to lock in a technical demo and validate integration points. BrightLeaf mentioned users across EU offices and one APAC pilot team — we want to make sure we propose demo windows that work across CET/ET/AEST and avoid DST pitfalls coming up in March.

I’m attaching the MAT questions you sent so Ava can prep. Quick ask: which time zone should we use as the primary reference (CET or ET)? title: SOC 2 Type II: snapshot checkpoint / bridge letter questions
preview:
From: Daniel Cho <daniel.cho@brightwave.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Feb 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Quick clarification on SOC 2 window and bridge letter

Hi Aisha,

We're preparing Brightwave's internal compliance notes ahead of our procurement sign-off and have a couple of quick clarifications on the SOC 2 Type II you shared. Two items from our audit team: 

1) Inclusive dates: your report lists the period ending 2026-12-31 with supporting evidence for January–February 2026 in some of the exports we received. Can you confirm which months are formally within the attested period (i.e., is Feb 2026 fully covered or treated as a partial/month-of-transition)?

2) Point-in-time snapshots: for controls that rely on system snapshots (user provisioning, key rotation, routing rules), what exact timestamp is used for the "point-in-time" captures? Are those taken at 00:00 UTC on the snapshot date, or in local PST? Our auditors prefer explicit UTC timestamps to avoid daylight savings ambiguity.

Finally, can Redwood provide a short bridge letter confirming (a) the attested period, (b) the exact timestamp convention for snapshots, and (c) any known scheduled maintenance windows during the period we should note? If you can draft that and share, we can route to our auditors for sign-off.

Thanks — appreciate the quick turnaround.

Daniel
Daniel Cho
Head of InfoSec 
Brightwave Therapeutics


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Cho <daniel.cho@brightwave.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Feb 2027 10:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick clarification on SOC 2 window and bridge letter

Hi Daniel — thanks for the good questions. I copied Marcus (security ops) to confirm the technical snapshot behavior; I'll also pull together a bridge-letter draft and attach the SOC 2 PDF you requested. 

High-level answers while Marcus confirms the logs:
- Attested period: Our Type II report covers the period 2026-01-01 through 2026-12-31 inclusive. Any evidence we provided labeled Jan/Feb 2026 were example exports from early 2026; the formal attestation period is the calendar year 2026.
- Snapshot timestamp convention: Marcus will confirm, but we use UTC for point-in-time exports by default.
- Maintenance windows: there was one scheduled maintenance on 2026-02-14 (UTC window 02:00–04:00) that we recommend you list as a known maintenance event.

Attachments: Redwood_SOC2_TypeII_Report_2026.pdf (redacted PII) and Bridge_Letter_Draft.docx — the bridge letter is a short template, please review and let us know any wording your auditors prefer.

Next steps: Marcus — can you confirm exact snapshot timestamp semantics (are snapshots taken at 00:00:00 UTC or at process-completion timestamps?), and whether exports for user provisioning are point-in-time dumps or rolling states?

Best,
Aisha
Aisha Rahman
Sales Engineering Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Daniel Cho <daniel.cho@brightwave.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Feb 2027 13:18:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick clarification on SOC 2 window and bridge letter

On Tue, Feb 16, 2027 at 10:03 AM Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com> wrote:
> Marcus — can you confirm exact snapshot timestamp semantics...?

Hi all — technical clarity below. Short answer: snapshots are UTC-aligned and the exports we hand to auditors are consistent point-in-time extracts. Details: 

- Snapshot cadence: Daily snapshots are taken at 00:00:00 UTC for stateful controls (user/provisioning, group memberships, KMS key metadata). The snapshot job checkpoints at that timestamp; any late writes after 00:00:00 are part of the next day's snapshot. 
- Rolling logs: Application and access logs are ingested in near real-time; for attestations we provide log slices matched to the snapshot timestamp (i.e., logs up to 00:00:00 UTC).
- Evidence exports: For provisioning controls we provide point-in-time CSV/JSON extracts (single-file) that represent the exact state at 00:00:00 UTC of the snapshot date.

One caveat: we had a scheduled platform maintenance on 2026-02-14 that required brief node restarts; that window was logged and excluded from some continuous availability metrics per auditor guidance. If QSR needs an explicit statement about that maintenance impact, we can add a short paragraph to the bridge letter.

Attaching a recommended phrasing below (Marcus) for Aisha to include in the bridge letter: 
\"Redwood Inference confirms that point-in-time evidence provided for the attested period was produced using daily UTC-aligned snapshots taken at 00:00:00 UTC. Rolling logs were correlated to those snapshots; any scheduled maintenance windows during the period are documented and specifically noted in our evidence package.\"

Let me know if your auditors require hashes or signed exports — we can provide S3 object hashes.

Marcus Lin
Principal Security Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Rachel Miller <rachel.miller@qsr-audit.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Cc: Daniel Cho <daniel.cho@brightwave.com>
Date: Tue, 16 Feb 2027 17:05:00 -0800
Subject: RE: Quick clarification on SOC 2 window and bridge letter

Thanks Aisha/Marcus — that's helpful and aligns with what we need. A few quick notes from the audit desk: 

- Confirmation: 2026-01-01 through 2026-12-31 will be treated as the attested period (inclusive). We will record Feb 2026 as within the attested year; the early-2026 example exports are fine as supplementary evidence.
- Timestamp: 00:00:00 UTC snapshots are acceptable. Please include the exact phrasing Marcus suggested in the bridge letter.
- Maintenance: we will note the 2026-02-14 maintenance window as a scheduled event and do not expect that to disqualify coverage, provided it's called out in the bridge letter.
- Minor edit: in section 2 of the draft bridge letter please change "excluded from continuous availability metrics" to "excluded from specific availability measurements for the maintenance window" — that wording is clearer for our report footnotes.

If you update the bridge letter with that wording, send back a final PDF and we can attach our signed acceptance (I'll return the signed acceptance as QSR_Audit_Acceptance_20270216.pdf).

Regards,
Rachel Miller
Lead Auditor, QSR Audit Services

Attachments: QSR_Audit_Acceptance_20270216.pdf (signed acceptance stub)

title: Bridge plan, go/no-go criteria, and cross-team commitments
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa@redwood.com>
Cc: Sarah Nguyen <sarah@atlashealth.com>
Date: Sun, 14 Feb 2027 09:02:00 -0800
Subject: Bridge plan, go/no-go criteria, and cross-team commitments

Team —

With QBR two weeks out, I ran a rapid assessment of our product/eng delivery readiness and attached a \"bridge plan\" with: a decision tree for go/no-go, owner-level commitments for the next two sprints, and rollback criteria for the launch path. High-level summary below and asks at the bottom.

Quick summary (TL;DR)
- Primary gating areas: feature parity for the Embed API, latency regression risk on >8k context, and infra capacity (Dedicated pools).
- Proposed go/no-go triggers: critical regressions in accuracy, >=10% latency SLO degradation for p95, or unresolved infra capacity shortfall >20% of forecast peak.
- Rollback criteria: if any of the above persist after an agreed 24h mitigation window, fail forward to the last stable model + feature flag.

Key open risks
- Engineering: KV cache regression when serving 8k seq workloads (unknown surface area in the quantized path).\
- Infra: Dedicated pool quotas show pinch points in eu-west during expected burst slices (see attached snapshot).
- Product: AtlasHealth requires a hard ETA for the privacy-edge integration for their prelaunch pilot.

A few concrete asks — please reply with owners and confirmations by EOD Monday (2027-02-15):

Quick summary (TL;DR)
- Primary gating areas: feature parity for the Embed API, latency regression risk on >8k context, and infra capacity (Dedicated pools).
- Proposed go/no-go triggers: critical regressions in accuracy, >=10% latency SLO degradation for p95, or unresolved infra capacity shortfall >20% of forecast peak.
- Rollback criteria: if any of the above persist after an agreed 24h mitigation window, fail forward to the last stable model + feature flag.

Key open risks
- Engineering: KV cache regression when serving 8k seq workloads (unknown surface area in the quantized path).\
- Infra: Dedicated pool quotas show pinch points in eu-west during expected burst slices (see attached snapshot).
- Product: AtlasHealth requires a hard ETA for the privacy-edge integration for their prelaunch pilot.

1) Confirm acceptance or propose edits to the go/no-go triggers above.

title: Meridian Labs: security playbook for private deployment (VPC / air-gap asks)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Alice Park <alice@meridian-labs.com>; secops@meridian-labs.com
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2027 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Meridian: post-call recap & asks — private deployment security playbook

Hi Alice / Meridian security team,

Thanks again for taking the time today — great discussion. Below is a concise recap of what we heard + the follow-ups we promised. Please correct anything that doesn't match your internal notes.

Key items we captured
- PII scope: customer names, emails, order IDs, and clinician notes (free text) will be sent to Redwood in encrypted form for embeddings and generation.
- Retention ask: Meridian requires 30-day ephemeral retention for request/response payloads on the pilot, with logs retained for 180 days for audit.
- Logging: request-level audit logs are required (who called, model version, request_id). No full-text retention beyond 30 days unless explicitly approved.
- Residency/egress: pilot will run in a Redwood Private VPC in us-east-1. No external egress to third-party endpoints; outbound to Meridian endpoints allowed over approved peering tunnels only.
- Air-gap expectation: for a future on-prem iteration there may be a soft air-gap requirement — discussing feasibility and timelines separately.

Open technical clarification items (action owners):
1) PII redaction options: can Redwood redact stored embeddings automatically? (Action: Redwood — Ben to confirm embedding retention options)
2) KV/prefetch logging: how much token-level metadata is kept by the runtime? (Action: Redwood — Marcus to provide runtime logging matrix)
3) Network controls: can we enforce deny-by-default egress and granular port rules on the dedicated VPC? (Action: Redwood — Ava to produce VPC stub + recommended firewall rules)

Next steps and timeline
- Redwood to share an initial VPC network playbook and a one-page security checklist (today EOD). Attachment: redwood-arch-vpc-stub.pdf (stub).
- Meridian to share SOC extract and any mandatory CLA language (by end of week).
- Schedule a 45-min technical POC walk-through next week to validate egress and routing.

If that looks right I can convert these into a one-page mutual action plan.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
Attachment: redwood-arch-vpc-stub.pdf (pdf)
Link: https://drive.redwood.com/s/meridian-vpc-playbook



From: Alice Park <alice@meridian-labs.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: secops@meridian-labs.com
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2027 09:03:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Meridian: post-call recap & asks — private deployment security playbook

Ava — thanks for the detailed recap. This matches our notes. A few inline items and a doc drop: see answers below.

On Tue, 10 Feb 2027 at 15:12, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Key items we captured
> - PII scope: customer names, emails, order IDs, and clinician notes (free text) will be sent to Redwood in encrypted form for embeddings and generation.

Confirming: clinician notes are sensitive PHI; Meridian will only send them after DPA and willing to support encrypted-in-transit + at-rest keys. We will provide a limited dataset for the pilot (no more than 10k records).

> - Retention ask: Meridian requires 30-day ephemeral retention for request/response payloads on the pilot, with logs retained for 180 days for audit.

We expect retention control to be enforceable by tenant/config. Is this configurable per-deployment? If not, we need to know the manual process.

> - Logging: request-level audit logs are required (who called, model version, request_id). No full-text retention beyond 30 days unless explicitly approved.

We will send an extract from our internal SOC checklist; attaching a short excerpt that lists required audit fields. Attachment: meridian-soc-extract.pdf

> Open technical clarification items
> 1) PII redaction options: can Redwood redact stored embeddings automatically? (Action: Redwood — Ben to confirm embedding retention options)

Question for Redwood: do embeddings count as stored telemetry (i.e., subject to the 30-day retention) or do you treat embeddings as derived artifacts with separate retention?

> 2) KV/prefetch logging: how much token-level metadata is kept by the runtime? (Action: Redwood — Marcus to provide runtime logging matrix)

We need detail on whether KV cache keys or prefixes could contain identifiable tokens. If there is any chance, we need a plan to salt or hash keys.

> 3) Network controls: can we enforce deny-by-default egress and granular port rules on the dedicated VPC? (Action: Redwood — Ava to produce VPC stub + recommended firewall rules)

Yes — we require deny-by-default and explicit allow-lists. Also need to know whether the VPC supports private endpoints only (no NAT) and how we validate no unexpected outbound connections during the POC.

I think a short technical POC walkthrough focused on egress proofing and retention config will answer most items.

Best,
Alice Park
Director, IT Security — Meridian Labs
Attachment: meridian-soc-extract.pdf (pdf)



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Alice Park <alice@meridian-labs.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2027 11:45:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Meridian: post-call recap & asks — private deployment security playbook

Thanks Alice — good clarifications. A few technical clarifications from the runtime/retention side (short):

- Embeddings: treated as derived artifacts. By default we retain embeddings for 30 days for cache effectiveness, but we can enable a tenant-level policy to purge embeddings immediately after generation for the pilot. That purge can be automated via lifecycle policy.
- Logging matrix (summary): we record request_id, calling principal (API key or mTLS identity), model id/version, latency, token counts. We do NOT persist full request/response payloads beyond configured retention.
- KV cache: keys are deterministic by prefix + hashed request id. We can enable salted hashing to reduce collision/identifiability risk — Marcus will provide exact schema.
- Retention enforcement: configurable per-deployment; for Private VPC we run a policy agent that enforces lifecycle rules.

Actionable: Marcus, can you drop the runtime logging matrix (field names) to the shared drive so Alice can validate against your SOC fields?

Ava — our recommended minimal firewall policy for the pilot (high level):
- Deny all outbound by default
- Allow egress to Meridian CIDR blocks on TCP/443 only, and to Redwood internal monitoring endpoints over an approved peering (if agreed)
- Block DNS to public resolvers; provide a private resolver over the peering

I attached a short checklist spreadsheet we typically use to validate pilot readiness. Attachment: private-deploy-checklist.xlsx

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Alice Park <alice@meridian-labs.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2027 09:24:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Meridian: post-call recap & asks — private deployment security playbook

Alice / team — following up with the runtime artifacts + final pre-POC checklist.

On Thu, 12 Feb 2027 at 11:45, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> - KV cache: keys are deterministic by prefix + hashed request id. We can enable salted hashing to reduce collision/identifiability risk — Marcus will provide exact schema.

Attached: a short runtime logging matrix (fields listed), and a minimal test plan we'll run during the POC to validate egress controls and retention enforcement. Summary here:

Runtime logging matrix (high level)
- event_time
- tenant_id (hashed)
- request_id
- model_id
- caller_identity (mTLS CN or API key id)
- token_count_in
- token_count_out
- action (generate/embedding)

No full-text bodies are persisted beyond retention windows. We can hash tenant_id with a salt you control for extra protection.

Pre-POC checklist (we will run together):
1) Validate VPC peering and route tables (no NAT, deny-by-default egress enforced).
2) Verify TLS mTLS endpoints and confirm mutual authentication for admin APIs.
3) Run synthetic calls that include PII placeholders and confirm purge at 30s / 30 days (configurable).
4) Execute port-scan from inside VPC to ensure no unexpected outbound sockets.
5) Confirm monitoring endpoints are restricted to Redwood IPs (if Meridian requires zero external endpoints we will route monitoring over the peering).

Can we lock in a 45-min slot on Wed 17 Feb 2027 at 14:00 ET for the POC walk-through? If that works for Meridian I'll send a calendar invite and a short runbook.

Thanks,
Marcus Lin
Director, Runtime Engineering — Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
Attachment: runtime-logging-matrix.csv (csv), private-deploy-checklist.xlsx (xlsx)
Related link: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/deal/MD-2027-0421

Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2027 11:45:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Meridian: post-call recap & asks — private deployment security playbook

Thanks Alice — good clarifications. A few technical clarifications from the runtime/retention side (short):

- Embeddings: treated as derived artifacts. By default we retain embeddings for 30 days for cache effectiveness, but we can enable a tenant-level policy to purge embeddings immediately after generation for the pilot. That purge can be automated via lifecycle policy.
- Logging matrix (summary): we record request_id, calling principal (API key or mTLS identity), model id/version, latency, token counts. We do NOT persist full request/response payloads beyond configured retention.
- KV cache: keys are deterministic by prefix + hashed request id. We can enable salted hashing to reduce collision/identifiability risk — Marcus will provide exact schema.
- Retention enforcement: configurable per-deployment; for Private VPC we run a policy agent that enforces lifecycle rules.

Actionable: Marcus, can you drop the runtime logging matrix (field names) to the shared drive so Alice can validate against your SOC fields?

Ava — our recommended minimal firewall policy for the pilot (high level):
- Deny all outbound by default
- Allow egress to Meridian CIDR blocks on TCP/443 only, and to Redwood internal monitoring endpoints over an approved peering (if agreed)
- Block DNS to public resolvers; provide a private resolver over the peering

I attached a short checklist spreadsheet we typically use to validate pilot readiness. Attachment: private-deploy-checklist.xlsx

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Alice Park <alice@meridian-labs.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2027 09:24:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Meridian: post-call recap & asks — private deployment security playbook

Alice / team — following up with the runtime artifacts + final pre-POC checklist.

On Thu, 12 Feb 2027 at 11:45, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> - KV cache: keys are deterministic by prefix + hashed request id. We can enable salted hashing to reduce collision/identifiability risk — Marcus will provide exact schema.

Attached: a short runtime logging matrix (fields listed), and a minimal test plan we'll run during the POC to validate egress controls and retention enforcement. Summary here:

Runtime logging matrix (high level)
- event_time
- tenant_id (hashed)
- request_id
- model_id
- caller_identity (mTLS CN or API key id)
- token_count_in
- token_count_out
- action (generate/embedding)

No full-text bodies are persisted beyond retention windows. We can hash tenant_id with a salt you control for extra protection.

Pre-POC checklist (we will run together):
1) Validate VPC peering and route tables (no NAT, deny-by-default egress enforced).
2) Verify TLS mTLS endpoints and confirm mutual authentication for admin APIs.
3) Run synthetic calls that include PII placeholders and confirm purge at 30s / 30 days (configurable).
4) Execute port-scan from inside VPC to ensure no unexpected outbound sockets.
5) Confirm monitoring endpoints are restricted to Redwood IPs (if Meridian requires zero external endpoints we will route monitoring over the peering).

Can we lock in a 45-min slot on Wed 17 Feb 2027 at 14:00 ET for the POC walk-through? If that works for Meridian I'll send a calendar invite and a short runbook.

Thanks,
Marcus Lin
Director, Runtime Engineering — Redwood Inference
marcus.lin@redwood.com
Attachment: runtime-logging-matrix.csv (csv), private-deploy-checklist.xlsx (xlsx)

title: Expanding workloads pricing — slab design for new product teams
preview:
From: Aisha Khan <aisha.khan@acme.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, procurement@acme.ai
Date: Wed, 10 Feb 2027 18:12:00 -0800
Subject: Pricing for adding 'Convo' product team (estimated 10B tokens/mo)

Hi Ben / Redwood team,

Thanks again for the demo today — the live routing and per-route cost visibility look promising for our next product, Convo. We expect the Convo team to start with ~2B tokens in month 1 and ramp to ~10B tokens/mo by month 4.

Procurement asked that we get a short pricing proposal that compares: 1) pay-as-you-go per-token rates, 2) an incremental commit for the Convo team (12-month term), and 3) any onboarding credits or promotional discounts for moving existing workloads into a higher commit tier. 

A few constraints from our side: we prefer hosted API for now, need SOC2 evidence and basic data isolation assurances, and want to understand how you price mixed workloads (chat + embeddings + rerank). Can you send a short one-pager and a spreadsheet example showing cost at 2B / 5B / 10B tokens?

Appreciate a quick turnaround — procurement would like a draft by EOD PT on Friday if possible.

Best,
Aisha Khan
Head of Platform Partnerships
Acme AI

(attachment: RFP_Q1_acme.pdf — attached to procurement thread)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Khan <aisha.khan@acme.ai>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Feb 2027 09:04:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Pricing for adding 'Convo' product team (estimated 10B tokens/mo)

Hi Aisha — great to meet earlier. I've put together a concise proposal and attached a spreadsheet that models three pricing paths: PAYG, incremental commit (monthly committed tokens billed at a discounted per-token rate), and a blended slab for mixed model usage. High-level notes below; see attached for full math.

Key proposal highlights:
- PAYG baseline: standard hosted per-token rates (generation vs embeddings priced separately).
- Incremental commit tiers for Convo (12-month term):
  * Tier A (0–1B incremental): $0.0045/token (generation-weighted)
  * Tier B (1–5B incremental): $0.0034/token
  * Tier C (5–15B incremental): $0.0026/token
- For mixed workloads, the spreadsheet shows a model-mix toggle (chat 70% / embeddings 20% / rerank 10%) — we price embeddings and rerank at lower unit rates in the sheet.
- Onboarding credit: one-time $25k credit applied to the first invoice for new team onboarding (documentation, initial telemetry support).
- Ramp protection: first 90 days we allow negative true-up up to 30% below commit to avoid over-penalizing early month variance.

I've attached: 20270211-acme-pricing-sheet.xlsx (detailed per-token math) and a short one-pager. If this direction looks reasonable I can loop in Marissa (pricing) and Rishi (accounts) to finalize commercial language and terms.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
(attachment: 20270211-acme-pricing-sheet.xlsx)

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 11 Feb 2027 10:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Pricing for adding 'Convo' product team (estimated 10B tokens/mo)

Ben — thanks for the quick draft. A couple of commercial guardrails I think will help close:
- Offer a 3-month staged ramp schedule (commit increases 0% -> 50% -> 100% over 90 days) with the onboarding credit tied to hitting the 50% milestone.
- Add a limited-time incentive: an extra 2% off the aggregated per-token spend if Acme signs a 12-month commit for the Convo team within 21 days of this proposal.
- For renewals, give them the option to convert unused committed tokens into a discounted buffer for the next quarter (makes finance happier).

Marissa — can you update the sheet with blended-per-token totals (including the 2% incentive row) and provide suggested phrasing for the SOW and billing cadence? Rishi, FYI — we may need a short exception on minimum commit granularity for multi-team customers.

—Lauren
Director, Strategic Accounts | Redwood Inference
(attachment: none)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 12 Feb 2027 08:37:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Pricing for adding 'Convo' product team (estimated 10B tokens/mo)

Thanks — updated spreadsheet attached with the staged ramp and the 2% sign-within-21-days incentive. I also split the per-token rates by model class to make the blended math obvious. Highlights:
- Blended per-token after 2% incentive (assuming Convo mix 70% gen / 20% embed / 10% rerank):
  * 2B tokens: $0.00385/token (~$7.7k)
  * 5B tokens: $0.00312/token (~$15.6k)
  * 10B tokens: $0.00268/token (~$26.8k)
- One-time onboarding credit: $25k (applies to first invoice once 50% of committed tokens are delivered).
- Additional operational concession: 30-day priority access to our onboarding engineers for the first sprint (helps reduce integration time).

Open question for procurement: does Acme require a separate line item for SOC2 evidence and data processing addendum (we can provide both; usually SOC2 evidence is included in standard enterprise pack but some customers ask for packaged artifacts)?

I've attached: 20270212-acme-pricing-sheet-updated.xlsx and a draft SOW (ACME_RFO_SOW.pdf). Please review and I'll prepare a one-pager and DocuSign-ready addendum after Aisha confirms model mix.

Marissa Cole
Principal Pricing Manager | Redwood Inference
(attachment: 20270212-acme-pricing-sheet-updated.xlsx, ACME_RFO_SOW.pdf)

From: Aisha Khan <aisha.khan@acme.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>

Thanks, team — this is helpful. Two quick clarifications: 1) the model mix for Convo will likely be ~60% chat/generation, 30% embeddings, 10% rerank; 2) procurement confirms SOC2 + DPA are required (please include certs and standard DPA language).

We like the staged ramp + onboarding credit approach. Procurement requests a single-sheet summary and a DocuSign package (SOW + commercial terms) so they can route internally. Can you also add a short clause clarifying that unused committed tokens can be banked as a credit for the next 90 days (subject to a 10% conversion fee)? That will help get finance comfortable.

If you can send the one-pager and DocuSign link by EOD PT today we can schedule a redline call tomorrow morning.

Aisha
Head of Platform Partnerships | Acme AI
(Procurement contact: procurement@acme.ai)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Khan <aisha.khan@acme.ai>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>

- We send the DocuSign package (SOW + commercial terms + DPA) by EOD PT today.
- Schedule a 30-minute redline call tomorrow at 9am PT to walk procurement through the banking clause and the staged ramp.
- Once signed, we apply the $25k onboarding credit on month-1 invoice and activate priority onboarding.
