Great questions — short answers and proposed schedule below. Full answers will be covered in the deep-dive; consider this a pre-read for legal/ops: 

- Derivative artifacts: embeddings and internal tensors are ephemeral by default; we do not persist embeddings to object storage unless a customer enables a vector store integration. Debug logs are opt-in and can be redacted/pseudonymized client-side.
- Audit logs: we can provide immutable access logs for any retention window; logs include actor, timestamp, request id.
- EU residency + KMS: supported in Dedicated/Private; hosted EU region supports customer-managed keys via partner KMS — we’ll include configuration options in the one-pager.

Proposed next steps and calendar:
1) Tech deep-dive: Fri 2027-01-22 @ 10:00-11:30 PST (Ben, Eng lead, Infra eng, Nisha, Louise, your infra/ops contact) — I’ll send invite.
2) Legal packet + one-pager: we’ll deliver a data-flow diagram, SOC2 artifact pointers, and a one-page residency & retention options doc by Wed 2027-01-20.
3) DocuSign: I’ll send a preliminary pilot SOW for signature to speed procurement after the technical confirmation.

DocuSign link (prelim SOW): https://www.docusign.net/en/projects/abc123-sign

Ben

title: SIG follow-up: responsibilities matrix for Hosted / Dedicated / Private
preview:
From: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@helixhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Helix Security <security@helixhealth.com>, procurement@helixhealth.com
Date: Sun, 10 Jan 2027 08:12:00 -0500
Subject: SIG questions: who owns controls for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private?

Ben —

Thanks for the SIG packet you shared during the vendor review. Our InfoSec team reviewed the draft and has a few follow-ups specific to deployment responsibilities: for each deployment mode (Hosted, Dedicated, Private/VPC/on-prem) we need a clear mapping of who is responsible for the following categories in your SIG answers: data encryption at rest, KMS management, network perimeter, audit logging retention, and patch management.

A couple concrete asks:
- Can you confirm whether Hosted customers can request custom audit log retention beyond the default? If yes, who operates the storage and what guarantees are provided?
- For Dedicated, does Helix get dedicated KMS or do you integrate with our KMS?

We need these clarifications to complete our SIG assessment and present to our procurement and security committee.

Thanks —
Anna Lopez
Sr. InfoSec Manager, Helix Health

--
Note: Attached is the section of our SOC/SIG checklist that references retention and KMS requirements (we'll share the full doc if needed).

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@helixhealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 10 Jan 2027 10:05:00 -05:00
Subject: Re: SIG questions: who owns controls for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private?

Anna —

From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
To: Anna Lopez <anna.lopez@helixhealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>

Date: Mon, 11 Jan 2027 09:30:00 -05:00
Subject: Re: SIG questions: who owns controls for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private?

Anna —

Following Ben's note, a few technical clarifications from infra:

1) Encryption & KMS
- Hosted: Redwood manages the disk encryption keys and the KMS. Data at rest is encrypted with provider-managed keys; we can enable customer-specific envelope keys for enterprise customers under a contract that includes dedicated key integration. In that case, Helix would provision and rotate keys in your KMS and we would integrate via a customer KMS endpoint.
- Dedicated: We provide an integration stub; customers can route KMS calls to their VPC KMS endpoint. Operationally, Redwood manages the runtime machines but does not have access to plaintext key material.
- Private/on-prem: Customer owns KMS entirely and operates within their network.

2) Audit logs & retention
- Hosted: We collect control-plane and application logs; default retention is 90 days. For customers requiring >90 days we support delivery of compressed logs to a customer-controlled S3 bucket (Helix-managed) or a secure transfer to your logging ingestion. Redwood does not assume long-term archival storage on behalf of the customer for Hosted unless contracted.
- Dedicated: Logs can be exported to a customer-managed bucket with retention controlled by the customer.
- Private: Customer controls retention wholly.

3) Network & patching
- Hosted: Redwood manages network controls and patching. We publish a monthly patch cadence and emergency CVE procedures; customers get notification and mitigations.
- Dedicated: Network boundary is shared — customer VPC + Redwood-managed subnets; patching handled by Redwood but scheduled maintenance windows are coordinated with customers.
- Private: Customer schedules and performs patching (we provide runbooks).

I've attached a simple diagram that maps data flows and responsibilities (diagram-deployment-modes.png). Note: the Hosted long-retention option requires procurement approval and an SOW to capture storage costs and SLAs.

-Jonas
Jonas Weber
Director, Infrastructure

Subject: Re: MAP: Finvest evaluation gates & retention carveouts

Aisha —

Appreciate the MAP draft. Quick clarifications from security:
- SOC 2: we need Type II covering production and the data pipeline components (storage/ingest). Ideally evidence and sample configs for data export masking by Jan end.
- Retention: default retention for customer data should be configurable. For EU customers we require EU residency or explicit contractual controls.
- Backups: Finvest policy is 180 days for backups; audit log retention we typically ask >= 365 days.
- Subprocessors: please provide the full list and a comms plan for any changes.

security@finvest.com


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Claire Huang <security@finvest.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-01-06T15:22:00-08:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Finvest evaluation gates & retention carveouts

Thanks Claire — helpful notes. Short answers and proposed adjustments to the MAP (attached Finvest-MAP-v2.xlsx):

- SOC 2 scope: Redwood can include production + data-pipeline components in the evidence bundle. We'll mark any staging-only controls as out-of-scope and provide compensating evidence where needed.
- Retention tiers (proposed default, configurable per-account):
  * Session/temp caches: 30 days (configurable)
  * Backups (restores): 90 days default, enterprise option to extend to 180 days
  * Audit logs: 365 days (we can export longer-term archives on request)
  * KV/cached model artifacts: 30 days by default; customers can opt into longer retention under contract

We note Finvest prefers 180-day backups by default — we can accommodate as an enterprise setting in the DPA/annex.

- EU residency: We offer region-pinning for data at the account level; for the POC we can route EU customer data to eu-central-1. We will document the network boundary and data flow diagram in the MAP evidence bucket.

- Subprocessors & pen test: attaching current Subprocessor-List-2027.csv and proposing our security engineering lead (Ben) to take the CAIQ walk-through. We're happy to schedule pen test coordination after the CAIQ and SOC 2 evidence review.

Proposed acceptance checkpoints (to be reflected in MAP v2):
1) CAIQ workbook delivered (2027-01-14) -> Finvest security review + 45-min walkthrough (target 2027-01-18)
2) Evidence bundle (SOC2 artifacts + dataflow diagram + subprocessors) delivered (2027-01-26) -> review window 10 business days
3) DPA redlines returned (by Finvest legal) and counter-signed DocuSign (target 2027-02-14)

Attachments: Finvest-MAP-v2.xlsx, Subprocessor-List-2027.csv

Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha@redwood.com


From: Marco Rossi <legal@finvest.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>, Claire Huang <security@finvest.com>
Date: 2027-01-08T09:40:00+01:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Finvest evaluation gates & retention carveouts

Aisha / Claire —

Reviewing the MAP v2 and the proposed retention tiers. A few legal redlines and questions attached (DPA-redlines-annotated.docx) — highlights below:

- EU residency clause: for any Finvest EU account we need an explicit Data Residency Addendum (Annex) that commits to processing within the EU for both primary data and backups.
- Retention: prefer 180 days for backups as default for Finvest; we can agree to 90 days for non-production. Please confirm whether backup replication to non-EU regions is avoidable when EU residency is required.
- Logs & fraud detection: Finvest needs the ability to retain certain raw logs for fraud analytics for up to 3 years; propose carveout language in the DPA with appropriate access controls and purpose limitation.
- Subprocessor notifications: require 30 days notice for new subprocessors and an option to review high-risk subprocessors.
- Pen test: please provide process for test scheduling, contact, and required approvals for external pentesters.

We will push back if EU residency for backups cannot be guaranteed — please confirm technical feasibility.

Regards,
Marco Rossi
General Counsel, Finvest Capital
legal@finvest.com

Marco / Claire —

Thanks for the redlines — technical confirmations below to support the DPA annex and MAP acceptance gates:

- EU residency: we support region pinning at the account level. When EU residency is selected we will ensure primary storage and automated backups remain in eu-central-1. Cross-region replication is disabled for accounts set to EU residency; manual exports remain possible under contract.
- Backup retention: default 90 days (enterprise option 180) — we can set 180 days as the default for Finvest under the enterprise terms you requested.
- Audit logs: stored 365 days in the logging cluster; we can provide export to a Finvest-owned archive for longer-term retention (up to 3 years) as a billable export service.
- Logs for fraud analytics: we can implement a scoped carveout with restricted access, purpose-limited retention, and encrypted-at-rest tagging. We'll include this language in the DPA annex.
- Subprocessors: attached Subprocessor-List-2027.csv. We will notify Finvest 30 days in advance for new subprocessors and provide expedited review for high-risk ones.
- Pen test: Redwood provides an onboarding packet and a security contact for scheduling (security-ops@redwood.com). We require a short authorization form; details in the packet.

Next steps:
- I'll upload a short data flow diagram and the CAIQ draft to the shared Drive by EOD tomorrow.
- Propose a 45-minute technical walkthrough on 2027-01-13 at 10:00am CET to go through CAIQ/SOC2 artifacts.
- Once Marco confirms the DPA annex language for EU residency & retention carveouts, we can exchange DocuSign URLs (target 2027-02-14).

- Subprocessors & pen test: attaching current Subprocessor-List-2027.csv and proposing our security engineering lead (Ben) to take the CAIQ walk-through. We're happy to schedule pen test coordination after the CAIQ and SOC 2 evidence review.

Proposed acceptance checkpoints (to be reflected in MAP v2):
1) CAIQ workbook delivered (2027-01-14) -> Finvest security review + 45-min walkthrough (target 2027-01-18)
2) Evidence bundle (SOC2 artifacts + dataflow diagram + subprocessors) delivered (2027-01-26) -> review window 10 business days
3) DPA redlines returned (by Finvest legal) and counter-signed DocuSign (target 2027-02-14)

Attachments: Finvest-MAP-v2.xlsx, Subprocessor-List-2027.csv

Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
aisha@redwood.com


From: Marco Rossi <legal@finvest.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>, Claire Huang <security@finvest.com>
Date: 2027-01-08T09:40:00+01:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Finvest evaluation gates & retention carveouts

Aisha / Claire —

Reviewing the MAP v2 and the proposed retention tiers. A few legal redlines and questions attached (DPA-redlines-annotated.docx) — highlights below:

- EU residency clause: for any Finvest EU account we need an explicit Data Residency Addendum (Annex) that commits to processing within the EU for both primary data and backups.
- Retention: prefer 180 days for backups as default for Finvest; we can agree to 90 days for non-production. Please confirm whether backup replication to non-EU regions is avoidable when EU residency is required.
- Logs & fraud detection: Finvest needs the ability to retain certain raw logs for fraud analytics for up to 3 years; propose carveout language in the DPA with appropriate access controls and purpose limitation.
- Subprocessor notifications: require 30 days notice for new subprocessors and an option to review high-risk subprocessors.
- Pen test: please provide process for test scheduling, contact, and required approvals for external pentesters.

We will push back if EU residency for backups cannot be guaranteed — please confirm technical feasibility.

Regards,
Marco Rossi
General Counsel, Finvest Capital
legal@finvest.com


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Marco Rossi <legal@finvest.com>, Claire Huang <security@finvest.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-01-12T16:45:00-08:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Finvest evaluation gates & retention carveouts

Marco / Claire —

Thanks for the redlines — technical confirmations below to support the DPA annex and MAP acceptance gates:

- EU residency: we support region pinning at the account level. When EU residency is selected we will ensure primary storage and automated backups remain in eu-central-1. Cross-region replication is disabled for accounts set to EU residency; manual exports remain possible under contract.
- Backup retention: default 90 days (enterprise option 180) — we can set 180 days as the default for Finvest under the enterprise terms you requested.
- Audit logs: stored 365 days in the logging cluster; we can provide export to a Finvest-owned archive for longer-term retention (up to 3 years) as a billable export service.
- Logs for fraud analytics: we can implement a scoped carveout with restricted access, purpose-limited retention, and encrypted-at-rest tagging. We'll include this language in the DPA annex.
- Subprocessors: attached Subprocessor-List-2027.csv. We will notify Finvest 30 days in advance for new subprocessors and provide expedited review for high-risk ones.
- Pen test: Redwood provides an onboarding packet and a security contact for scheduling (security-ops@redwood.com). We require a short authorization form; details in the packet.

Next steps:
- I'll upload a short data flow diagram and the CAIQ draft to the shared Drive by EOD tomorrow.
- Propose a 45-minute technical walkthrough on 2027-01-13 at 10:00am CET to go through CAIQ/SOC2 artifacts.
- Once Marco confirms the DPA annex language for EU residency & retention carveouts, we can exchange DocuSign URLs (target 2027-02-14).

Attachments: SOC2-TypeII-Report.pdf, Subprocessor-List-2027.csv

Ben Carter

title: MedSolve trial charter + observation policy and rubric
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay.reddy@medsolve.com>, Megan Li <megan.li@medsolve.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 10 Jan 2027 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: MedSolve — post-demo recap and proposed trial charter

Hi Sanjay / Megan —

Thanks again for taking the demo earlier today. Quick recap while it’s fresh and a proposed lightweight charter for the trial so we’re all aligned before kickoff.

Call highlights:
- Primary goal: reduce time-to-insight for claims-review triage by surfacing likely high-priority claims and suggested rationale (target: 50% reduction in human triage time).
- Data scope: anonymized open claims for two lines of business (auto & home), ~10K events over 30 days for shadowing.
- Success criteria discussed: precision at top-5 should be >= 0.75 for flagged items, and average latency per request under 350ms p50 in your staging network.

Proposed trial charter (2 weeks):
1) Shadow mode (online observation) against a rolling 7-day window for production-like latency measurements.
2) Parallel offline evals on a held-out dataset to measure precision/recall against curated labels and to validate edge-case behavior.
3) Acceptance gates: top-5 precision >= 0.75 AND no critical privacy flags in 1000-sample audit.

Owners / roles:
- Redwood: Ben (primary), Marissa (ops lead for setup + instrumentation)
- MedSolve: Sanjay (product), Megan (data / labels)

Immediate next steps:
- Sanjay / Megan to confirm the anonymized dataset drop by EOD Wednesday (2027-01-13).
- Redwood to share a trial charter PDF and a sample eval rubric for your review (attached).
- If shadow-only is preferred we’ll tighten sampling to limit PII exposure; if you want a combined approach we’ll run both shadow and offline scoring in parallel.

I’ve attached a short charter and the evaluation rubric spreadsheet — please review and tell us which observation method you prefer (\"shadow only\" or \"shadow + offline\") and any changes to the acceptance gates.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0122

Attachments: trial-charter.pdf (application/pdf), medsolve-eval-rubric.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

From: Sanjay Reddy <sanjay.reddy@medsolve.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Megan Li <megan.li@medsolve.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 11 Jan 2027 09:38:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MedSolve — post-demo recap and proposed trial charter

Thanks Ben — good recap. A couple of clarifications from our side: 

1) Dataset timing: we can deliver an anonymized 30-day extract by Thursday 2027-01-14, not Wednesday — hope that’s OK.
2) Observation preference: we want shadow + offline in parallel. Shadow for latency/safety and offline to measure precision on labeled cases.
3) Acceptance gate tweak: can we use precision@3 >= 0.7 as a secondary gate (we prioritize catching top few hits for immediate triage).

Also looping in procurement (procurement@medsolve.com) for the SOW and any formality on data handling.

If you can adjust the charter with the Wednesday->Thursday change and add precision@3 as an explicit secondary gate, we’re good to proceed.

— Sanjay
Sanjay Reddy
Head of Product, MedSolve
sanjay.reddy@medsolve.com

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 11 Jan 2027 12:05:00 -0800
Subject: Internal — resource plan & eval approach recommendation

Ben / Marissa — quick internal note after Sanjay’s reply.

Recommendation (based on their ask for \"shadow + offline\"):
- Run shadow for 10 business days to gather latency + safety signals; use a 10% traffic sample to minimize risk. Shadow will give us p50/p95 in their network and surface any production-only failure modes.
- Parallel offline eval on the full 10K sample with the rubric they agreed to; we should compute precision@3 and precision@5, recall and an error-type breakdown (false positives by category).
- Add a small human-audit checkpoint (100 random shadowed recommendations) to validate privacy filters and hallucination rates.

Ops items for Marissa:
- Infra: provision a staging GPU pod in their VPC or run private connector — estimate 4 hours to configure and 2 hours for a smoke run.
- Instrumentation: enable per-request tracing and token-level cost logging; send us the sample keys and an endpoint for webhook callbacks.

I'll draft a revised charter reflecting Thursday drop and explicit gates (precision@3 >= 0.7; precision@5 >= 0.75; latency p50 < 350ms). Ben — do you want me to produce the short acceptance checklist and the audit plan?

Thanks,
Lauren
Lauren Bishop
Senior Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference
lauren.bishop@redwood.com

preview:
From: Elena Park <elena.park@cobaltedge.com>

Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@cobaltedge.com>
Date: Sun, 03 Jan 2027 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Interim assurance note request — cover gap between SOC 2 period and procurement deadline

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We completed our review of the SOC 2 Type II report you provided that covers controls through 2023-11-30. Our procurement and security review teams are asking for a brief interim assurance / bridge statement to cover the gap from the report end date to the current date.

Specifically, we need:
- A one-page bridge statement confirming there have been no material changes to the design or operation of controls relevant to security, availability, or confidentiality since 2023-11-30, or a short description of any changes and compensating controls.
- A copy of the SOC 2 Type II report (if available in PDF)
- A short evidence pack: system architecture diagram, change control policy excerpt, current roles & access matrix (redacted), and confirmation of log retention policy for auth/login events.

Our vendor review deadline is Jan 14, so if you can send a draft by Jan 7 that would be ideal. We can accept a DocuSign-signed statement or an emailed PDF.

Let me know if you need clarification on scope or specific phrasing.

Thanks,
Elena Park
Sr. Security Program Manager
Cobalt Edge
elena.park@cobaltedge.com

title: Vireo: Log retainability artefact delivery plan
preview:
From: Jane Park <jane.park@vireotherapeutics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-12-20T09:14:00-08:00
Subject: Vireo: log retention and proof-of-delete details

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking this on. Our external SOC 2 auditor has asked for customer-specific evidence that demonstrates Redwood's deletion path and log retention controls for tenant-scoped debug traces. Specifically they requested: 1) excerpt from your retention policy showing default TTL for ephemeral inference logs, 2) a walk-through or runbook for 'proof-of-delete' (how you validate an erasure request finished), and 3) a redacted example of deletion logs or API audit entries showing an executed erase for a tenant (timestamps and flow, redacted PII).

We also have CAIQ and SIG mapping questions around whether debug traces are considered \"ephemeral\" vs. \"audit\" data in your environment and whether you support customer-requested retention exceptions (we may need a 90-day forensics hold for a subset of tenants). Could you confirm whether Redwood can provide a signed attestation for a temporary retention exception, and what controls you put around privileged access to retained traces?

We need the package by end of next week (2026-12-29) for the audit. Happy to hop on a quick call if useful.

preview:
From: Alice Monroe <alice@melterco.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-27T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Coupa onboarding error: cannot select Tax Entity Type - PO on hold

Hi Ben,

We received the invite to MelterCo's supplier portal this morning but when I try to confirm our company details the form throws a validation error on the "Tax Entity Type" field (see attached). The UI shows: "No ID mapping found for tax_entity_type" and prevents saving — which means the PO referenced in the invite (PO# 12345) is stuck.

I attached a screenshot (coupa_error_20261227.png). Our procurement team is waiting on the PO to be released; can you advise next steps or offer a workaround?

Thanks,
Alice Monroe
Vendor Onboarding
MelterCo
procurement@melterco.com

--
Attachment: coupa_error_20261227.png (image/png)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alice Monroe <alice@melterco.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-27T10:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Coupa onboarding error: cannot select Tax Entity Type - PO on hold

On Mon, Dec 27, 2026 at 9:12 AM Alice Monroe <alice@melterco.com> wrote:
> We received the invite to MelterCo's supplier portal this morning but when I try to confirm our company details the form throws a validation error on the "Tax Entity Type" field (see attached).
>
> The UI shows: "No ID mapping found for tax_entity_type" and prevents saving — which means the PO referenced in the invite (PO# 12345) is stuck.

Alice — thanks for the screenshot, that helps. I'm looping in Marissa from onboarding and Evelyn from procurement ops to triage.

Quick asks so we can move faster:
- Can you confirm MelterCo's vendor ID in your system (if you have it) or the full legal name and tax ID?
- If possible, please attach the exact invite link (or the full URL) — sometimes payload changes are visible in the link params.

Work plan:
1) If this is a mapping issue on our side we can create a temporary mapping or set the tax_entity_type server-side and then resend the invite.
2) If it's a Coupa template mismatch, Marissa will advise the small change to the invite.

I've attached the PO for reference (PO_12345.pdf).

I'll update you as soon as onboarding confirms the approach.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0123

--
Attachment: PO_12345.pdf (application/pdf)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-28T08:30:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Coupa onboarding error: cannot select Tax Entity Type - PO on hold

Ben / team,

Thanks for the context and the screenshot. This is a fairly common symptom when the portal's drop-down is expecting a Coupa-side enumerated id but our vendor_profile mapping uses a different canonical (string) value. In short: payload mismatch between our invite template and Coupa's tenant mapping.

Two recommended fixes (fastest first):

Option A — Server-side patch (recommended, low risk, quick):
- Provide MelterCo's vendor identifier (or consent to me using company legal name + tax_id)
- I will add a temporary mapping entry in the onboarding service so the incoming tax_entity_type value resolves to the expected enum id
- Resend invite; supplier will then complete form and PO will flow

Option B — Template change (requires Coupa template edit):
- Update the invite payload to send the enum id instead of the human-readable string
- This requires a small Coupa-side edit and a redeploy of the template; ETA ~ 1 business day

If you choose A, note that this is a manual mapping that we keep for 30 days and then migrate to the canonical mapping table. I attached step-by-step notes for the patch (mapping_patch_instructions.md).

What I need from MelterCo: the vendor identifier they see in their portal or the legal/tax info we can use to generate the mapping.

Marissa Cole
Onboarding Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

--
Attachment: mapping_patch_instructions.md (text/markdown)

To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-12-28T09:15:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Coupa onboarding error: cannot select Tax Entity Type - PO on hold

MelterCo vendor id: MEL-99821
Legal name: MelterCo, Inc.

Alice Monroe

title: ISO + FedRAMP sprint: audit playbook scope & sample requests
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Lisa Nguyen <lisa.nguyen@northbridge.com>, Infra Audit Team <infra-audit@northbridge.com>
Cc: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 22 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: ISO + FedRAMP sprint: audit playbook scope & sample requests

Hi Lisa / Northbridge audit team,

Thanks for the heads-up about the pre-audit window. We want to make sure the first pass of our playbook aligns to your expectations so we can produce the right samples without rework. A few clarifying asks from our side: 

- Can you confirm the control families you want sampled for ISO 27001 vs. FedRAMP? (e.g., access control, crypto/key mgmt, change management).
- For FedRAMP scoping: are we in-scope for CSP-hosted data only, or also consultant/staging environments?
- Desired sample window for logs and artifacts (30 / 90 / 180 days)?
- Will you require SIG/CAIQ mapping for the control set, or are we to provide CAIQ directly?
- Any auditor-specific evidence formats (CSV exports, signed attestations, PDF runbooks) you prefer?

I've attached a draft ISO scope and our current control matrix to help you pinpoint gaps. Olga and Rishi are copied to pick up evidence tasks on our side.

Proposed next steps: if it helps, we can schedule a 30-minute call early next week to align owners and delivery dates.

Attachments (stubs): ISO_scope_draft.pdf (draft), control_matrix.xlsx (mapping)

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0199



From: Lisa Nguyen <lisa.nguyen@northbridge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Infra Audit Team <infra-audit@northbridge.com>
Cc: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 23 Dec 2026 10:07:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ISO + FedRAMP sprint: audit playbook scope & sample requests

Hi Ben — thanks for the helpful artifacts. A few clarifications from our audit ops perspective: 

- Primary control families we will sample for both ISO and FedRAMP: Access Controls (AC), Cryptography (SC-13 equivalents), Configuration Management (CM), and Logging/Monitoring (AU). We may request a couple of business-continuity artifacts for ISO (plans, table-top minutes).
- For FedRAMP, we only need production tenant data in-scope; please confirm there are no shared admin accounts between prod and pre-prod.
- Sample window: 90 days for logs; 12 months for policy artifacts and control attestations.
- CAIQ is preferred as the canonical intake for the third-party questionnaire; SIG mapping is optional but helpful.
- Evidence formats: CSV/JSON exports for logs (we'll ingest), PDF for runbooks, and signed spreadsheets for access review summaries.

One critical question: can your platform provide KMS key rotation logs that indicate key ID, rotation timestamp, and operator identity? This is a hard ask for FedRAMP.

We'd like to run an initial alignment call the week of Jan 4. I can do Jan 5 or Jan 6 in the morning PT.

Thanks,
Lisa Nguyen
Senior IT Auditor, Northbridge Bank
lisa.nguyen@northbridge.com



From: Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Cc: Lisa Nguyen <lisa.nguyen@northbridge.com>, Infra Audit Team <infra-audit@northbridge.com>
Date: Thu, 24 Dec 2026 13:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ISO + FedRAMP sprint: audit playbook scope & sample requests

Ben / Rishi — looping in with quick internal plan based on Lisa's asks (see excerpt below).

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@datavine.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 22 Dec 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call: retention alignment & path for PHI/non-PHI endpoints

Hi Sanjay — thanks again for the time earlier. Short recap and a proposed path so we can converge quickly: 

Call highlights (from our side):
- Primary ask: Datavine needs zero-persistence for PHI endpoints (no raw prompt storage) and tighter controls for any PII.
- Secondary: team is okay with short-term aggregated logs for analytics if redacted and anonymized.
- Residency: production for PHI must run in us-east-2; non-PHI workloads can be multi-region.
- Procurement note: you asked whether we can offer customer-managed keys and evidence for encryption at rest.

Two pragmatic options we recommend (technical + contract impact):
1) No-persist PHI streams (preferred): runtime-only inference with KV cache TTL = 1 hour, no write to persistent blob store, request-level redaction pipeline, and optional webhook audit with hashed identifiers. Requires Private deployment or Dedicated with strict tenancy.
2) Short-retain hybrid: keep encrypted, redacted request/response logs for 7 days for troubleshooting + aggregated analytics (PII removed at ingestion). This is simpler operationally but not acceptable for PHI endpoints per your compliance note.

Attachments: redwood-short-retain-options.pdf (technical breakdown) and a draft mutual-action-plan.

Asks / next steps:
- Can you confirm that PHI must be 0-persist (no logs) for the three endpoints discussed?
- If non-PHI can use a 7-day redacted window, confirm that as well.
- Please share the compliance checklist (if you have one) and any Legal contacts to loop into the BAA conversation.

I’ve looped in Marissa (solutions eng) and Monica (privacy) to scope the runbook and compliance evidence. Happy to schedule a technical deep-dive tomorrow or Thursday.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0199

Attachment stubs: redwood-short-retain-options.pdf (5 pages, diagrams); mutual-action-plan-datavine.xlsx (MAP, 3 tabs)

From: Sanjay Rao <sanjay@datavine.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Legal Team <legal@datavine.ai>
Date: Tue, 22 Dec 2026 16:18:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call: retention alignment & path for PHI/non-PHI endpoints

Ben — appreciated the clear options. Quick clarifications from our side: 

- PHI requirement: yes, any endpoint that processes PHI must be configured with zero-persistence. We need cryptographic assurance that raw prompts and model outputs are not persisted beyond in-memory caches. Is the 1-hour TTL on KV cache still writable to disk in some failure modes? We cannot accept disk-backed fallthrough for PHI.

- Non-PHI analytics: we can tolerate up to 30 days of redacted logs for usage analytics, but those logs must be redacted before they land in any persistent store.

- Keys & evidence: our security team needs to see how keys are managed. Can Redwood support external KMS (AWS KMS customer-managed keys) with HSM-backed keys and provide a KMS binding document? Also, we will want a BAA; please confirm your process and timeline.

Looping in Legal on a first pass. I’ve attached our internal compliance checklist — it’ll highlight the items you’ll see in the next email from Legal.

—Sanjay

Head of Platform Security | Datavine AI

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>

Date: Wed, 23 Dec 2026 08:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call: retention alignment & path for PHI/non-PHI endpoints

Sanjay — thanks for the quick clarifications, and thanks for the checklist. Answers inline and proposed next steps: 

1) Disk-backed fallback: Redwood’s runtime has a strict policy toggle for PHI streams — when "no-persist" is enabled, the system disables any write paths to disk for request/response payloads. KV cache remains in RAM only and is never flushed to disk; in the rare case of host failure we fail closed (requests error) rather than persist. We will provide an architectural note and an acceptance test you can run.

2) Short-retain window: we can support either 7-day or 30-day redacted retention for non-PHI. Operationally we recommend 7 days as a starting point, but we can accommodate your 30-day analytics ask if you need it. All PII redaction happens at the ingress pipeline; redaction rules are configurable and we keep a redaction audit log (hash-only) for debugging.

3) KMS / HSM: we support customer-managed keys (AWS KMS CMKs) for both Dedicated and Private deployments. For Private we can also support HSM-based KMS via CloudHSM. We’ll attach a KMS binding and key-handling doc in the next message from Monica.

4) BAA: Redwood can execute a standard BAA; I’ll loop Legal internally and will coordinate a first-draft with your Legal team.

Next steps:
- Marissa will prepare a short technical runbook showing the PHI "no-persist" toggle and an acceptance checklist.
- Monica will provide the KMS/key handling artifacts and sample audit output.
- Propose a 45-minute joint session next Tuesday (12/29) to walk through the runbook and to have Legal sync on the BAA redlines. Please confirm availability.

Marissa / Monica — any additional tech artifacts you want attached here?

Thanks,
Ben

title: Follow-up: packaging discount math and min-charge on Dec bill
preview:
From: Alex Soto <alex.soto@lumenretail.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Lumen Retail Billing <billing@lumenretail.com>
Date: Tue, 1 Dec 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Dec invoice: packaging discount + min-charge — need line-item help

Hi Ben,

We just received the Dec invoice (attached) and there are a couple of line items that don’t match how we understood the packaging discount to apply. Two quick questions:

1) The invoice shows a "minimum charge" of 75k tokens for the month even though our usage for the month (per our dashboard) summed to ~62k billed tokens after deduping. Is the minimum applied before or after discount?

2) How are streaming calls and long-context sessions bucketed? We stream a lot of short pings (100–400 token responses) plus occasional very long retrieval prompts that keep a 16k context open. Does the per-call minimum apply to each stream chunk, or do you aggregate per-session for packaging customers?

Can you send a simple breakdown (per API call id or time window) showing how you calculated the billed tokens and where the floor/rounding steps were applied? I attached the invoice and an export of our API usage (CSV) — happy to share more if it helps.

Best,
Alex
Alex Soto
Head of ML Integrations, Lumen Retail
alex.soto@lumenretail.com
attachments: Lumen_Q4_invoice.pdf (pdf), api_usage_export_dec.csv (csv)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Soto <alex.soto@lumenretail.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Dec 2026 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Dec invoice: packaging discount + min-charge — need line-item help

Alex — thanks for the note and the attachments, I pulled the invoice into our billing team for a quick pass. High-level: there are two places rounding/minimums can affect the final invoice for packaged SKUs, and both likely explain what you’re seeing. I put the short answers first and then an example.

Short answers:
- Discount ordering: packaging discounts are applied after we compute the billed token quantity for each invoice line item (we don’t discount the minimum itself). The minimum is evaluated at the invoice line level before the percent discount is applied.
- Aggregation window: for customers on a packaged (commit) SKU we aggregate requests into hourly invoice windows per route/model, then round each window to the SKU quantum (we use 1,000-token invoice quanta for packaged tiers).
- Streaming vs per-call minimum: we enforce a per-call micro-minimum of 32 tokens for streaming RPCs (to avoid excessive microbilling), but the hourly aggregation can absorb those and still round up to the 1,000-token invoice quantum.

Example (from your exported calls):
- Raw per-call tokens in hour 2026-12-03T11:00–12:00: 12 calls at 120 tokens = 1,440 tokens total.
- Apply per-call micro-minimums: each call already >32 so unchanged.
- Aggregate hourly total: 1,440 tokens -> invoice quantum rounds to 2,000 (round-up to nearest 1,000).
- Apply packaging discount (you have 20% on this tier): 2,000 * 0.8 = 1,600 billed tokens on that line.

So if you look only at the dashboard raw token sum (62k) you’ll see the smaller number; the invoice reflects our quanta/aggregation + ordering above which can push the invoice higher in months of bursty short calls.

I attached a quick calculator (token_calc_example.xlsx) showing the step-by-step for the two busiest days you sent. If you’d like, we can run a one-off re-calculation for Dec using a tighter aggregation policy (e.g., 15-minute windows instead of 1-hour) and see the delta — that’s normally how we do a one-time true-up when the quanta materially impacts a pilot.

Next steps I propose:
1) I’ll run the re-calculation and share a per-hour breakdown.
2) If there’s an overcharge because we applied the quantum/ordering in a way that contradicts the signed packaging language, we’ll credit you the delta for Dec.
3) We can add a packaging amendment to the deal to lock in a 15-minute aggregation window for Lumen going forward (avoids the large rounding up). I attached a short amendment draft to start.

Cc’ing Marissa to coordinate the backfill and Karthik for pricing amendment ops.

Subject: Dec invoice: packaging discount + min-charge — need line-item help

Hi Ben,

We just received the Dec invoice (attached) and there are a couple of line items that don’t match how we understood the packaging discount to apply. Two quick questions:

1) The invoice shows a "minimum charge" of 75k tokens for the month even though our usage for the month (per our dashboard) summed to ~62k billed tokens after deduping. Is the minimum applied before or after discount?

2) How are streaming calls and long-context sessions bucketed? We stream a lot of short pings (100–400 token responses) plus occasional very long retrieval prompts that keep a 16k context open. Does the per-call minimum apply to each stream chunk, or do you aggregate per-session for packaging customers?

Can you send a simple breakdown (per API call id or time window) showing how you calculated the billed tokens and where the floor/rounding steps were applied? I attached the invoice and an export of our API usage (CSV) — happy to share more if it helps.

Best,
Alex
Alex Soto
Head of ML Integrations, Lumen Retail
alex.soto@lumenretail.com
attachments: Lumen_Q4_invoice.pdf (pdf), api_usage_export_dec.csv (csv)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Soto <alex.soto@lumenretail.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 3 Dec 2026 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Dec invoice: packaging discount + min-charge — need line-item help

Alex — thanks for the note and the attachments, I pulled the invoice into our billing team for a quick pass. High-level: there are two places rounding/minimums can affect the final invoice for packaged SKUs, and both likely explain what you’re seeing. I put the short answers first and then an example.

Short answers:
- Discount ordering: packaging discounts are applied after we compute the billed token quantity for each invoice line item (we don’t discount the minimum itself). The minimum is evaluated at the invoice line level before the percent discount is applied.
- Aggregation window: for customers on a packaged (commit) SKU we aggregate requests into hourly invoice windows per route/model, then round each window to the SKU quantum (we use 1,000-token invoice quanta for packaged tiers).
- Streaming vs per-call minimum: we enforce a per-call micro-minimum of 32 tokens for streaming RPCs (to avoid excessive microbilling), but the hourly aggregation can absorb those and still round up to the 1,000-token invoice quantum.

Example (from your exported calls):
- Raw per-call tokens in hour 2026-12-03T11:00–12:00: 12 calls at 120 tokens = 1,440 tokens total.
- Apply per-call micro-minimums: each call already >32 so unchanged.
- Aggregate hourly total: 1,440 tokens -> invoice quantum rounds to 2,000 (round-up to nearest 1,000).
- Apply packaging discount (you have 20% on this tier): 2,000 * 0.8 = 1,600 billed tokens on that line.

So if you look only at the dashboard raw token sum (62k) you’ll see the smaller number; the invoice reflects our quanta/aggregation + ordering above which can push the invoice higher in months of bursty short calls.

I attached a quick calculator (token_calc_example.xlsx) showing the step-by-step for the two busiest days you sent. If you’d like, we can run a one-off re-calculation for Dec using a tighter aggregation policy (e.g., 15-minute windows instead of 1-hour) and see the delta — that’s normally how we do a one-time true-up when the quanta materially impacts a pilot.

Next steps I propose:
1) I’ll run the re-calculation and share a per-hour breakdown.
2) If there’s an overcharge because we applied the quantum/ordering in a way that contradicts the signed packaging language, we’ll credit you the delta for Dec.
3) We can add a packaging amendment to the deal to lock in a 15-minute aggregation window for Lumen going forward (avoids the large rounding up). I attached a short amendment draft to start.

Cc’ing Marissa to coordinate the backfill and Karthik for pricing amendment ops.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0182
attachments: token_calc_example.xlsx (xlsx), packaging_amendment_draft.docx

title: Intermittent P99 bifurcation impacting Checkoutly - urgent ops sync

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: ops@brightcart-internal@example.com
Date: Tue, 22 Dec 2026 09:06:00 -0800
Subject: Intermittent P99 bifurcation impacting Checkoutly - urgent ops sync

Team —

We started seeing a split in the p99 distribution on /v1/generate-checkout (Checkoutly tenant) at ~08:50 PST. Two clear modes: a ~220ms mode and a tailing mode hitting 3.2s for ~1.8% of requests over the last 15m. Throughput is nominal (QPS steady), but the high tail correlates with large context-length requests from a small subset of users (>1k tokens). Attached is the p99_bifurcation_graph.png and a small sample of logs (checkpoint_sample_logs.zip).

Immediate asks:
- SRE (Soojin / Karthik): can we triage whether this is kernel scheduling (multi-tenant eviction) vs KV-cache warmup? I suspect eviction because we see sudden KV miss spikes.
- Product (Marissa): need a short customer note and an ask for permission to route Checkoutly to the warm-path cluster temporarily (estimated 15–30% cost uplift) while we confirm root cause.
- I will coordinate the customer bridge if you want me to (available now).

Visibility: target a 30-min bridge; if SRE can confirm a mitigation (warm-path routing or temporary QC throttle) quickly, we avoid rolling out broader fallbacks.

Ben
Ben Carter
Sr. Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0191

Attachments: p99_bifurcation_graph.png, checkpoint_sample_logs.zip


From: Soojin Lee <soojin@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 22 Dec 2026 09:14:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent P99 bifurcation impacting Checkoutly - urgent ops sync

Ben — thanks for flagging and for the artifacts. Quick read: the KV miss rate spike in the logs lines up with a garbage-collection window on host fleet H-17 and increased kernel preemption on the same hosts.

Short-term mitigation options I see (ordered by speed to deploy):
1) Route Checkoutly traffic to warm-path pool (requires product sign-off, ~5m routing change).
2) Enable conservative KV-prefetch for Checkoutly (experimental, code flip, ~20m with rollout).
3) Canary a small quantized model instance for oversized contexts (complex, ~1–2h).

I'll start a short bridge and check host health; Karthik, can you confirm whether we have spare warm-path capacity in us-west-2? If not, we can spin reserved-instances but that will take longer.

I approve drafting a customer note that mentions we're investigating and may route traffic to a warm-path temporarily. Marissa, can you draft language and include cost/impact?

Soojin
Soojin Lee
Senior SRE, Redwood Inference
soojin@redwood.com

title: Startup Slate — one-off credit carveout and onboarding pricing question
preview:
From: Maya Cheng <maya@startup-slate.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 19 Dec 2026 14:22:00 -0800
Subject: Startup Slate — one-off credit carveout and onboarding pricing question

Hi Chloe,

Hope you’re well — quick intro, I run partnerships at Startup Slate, we run a six-week accelerator for pre-seed / seed teams (12 companies in the winter cohort). We loved Redwood’s benchmarks from the last demo and think the platform could be a great fit for several of our grads.

We have a couple of asks and were wondering if Redwood could consider a one-off carveout to your standard promo rules:
- Can you provide a small pool of free credits for the cohort (12 teams) for the first 3 months? We’re thinking something meaningful per team so they can prototype LLM features without immediately jumping to dedicated capacity.
- Our partners often want the ability to convert leftover credits into a discount on a reserved/dedicated commitment within 6 months. Is that something you’d consider (rather than letting unused credits expire)?
- Lastly, any flexibility on stacking promo credits with marketplace reserved discounts would make a big difference for our sponsors.

Timeline: we need to publish the partner benefit on Monday (Dec 22) — any chance we can get a candidate offer or sign-off before EOD Monday? Happy to hop on a 20-min call. I’ve attached a brief cohort summary.

Thanks,
Maya Cheng
Program Lead — Startup Slate
maya@startup-slate.com
+1 415-555-0132

Attachment: Startup_Slate_Cohort_Summary.pdf (pdf)


From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Maya Cheng <maya@startup-slate.com>
Date: Sun, 20 Dec 2026 09:10:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Startup Slate — one-off credit carveout and onboarding pricing question

Ben / Laura — looping you in. Maya is asking for a cohort-level carveout and the ability to convert unused credits to a committed discount later. She needs a candidate offer before Monday.

Quick thoughts:
- Promo stacking: our standard policy disallows stacking full promotional credits with reserved-commit discounts, but we have some precedent for a "convertibility" clause where credits can be converted to a one-time committed discount if converted within a fixed window.
- Scale: 12 teams x proposed credit amount is the key ask — we should counter with a structured offer that preserves unit economics.
- Legal/Finance: will need a short MSA addendum for any convertibility / invoicing rules.

Suggested candidate offer to put on the table (for discussion):
1) 100k token-equivalent credits per team (active for 90 days) for the cohort (pilot issuance; non-transferable between cohorts).
2) Convertibility: any unused credits can be converted into a one-time 20% discount on a single month of Dedicated capacity if purchaser signs a minimum 3-month Dedicated commitment within 90 days. Conversion must be requested via Sales and will be administratively applied as a credit line item on the first invoice.
3) Marketplace stacking: allow combination with marketplace reserved pricing, but limit to one convertibility per account (no double-dipping on the discount).

If we agree to this structure, Ben — can you run a quick cost model (COGS impact + revenue delta)? Laura — curious about invoicing cadence and tax/treatment implications. I’m fine to share a brief offer PDF with Maya once we confirm wording.

Chloe
Director, Partner Programs
Redwood Inference

Attachment: proposed-packaging-options.xlsx (internal stub)

On Sat, Dec 19, 2026 at 02:22 PM Maya Cheng <maya@startup-slate.com> wrote:
> Hi Chloe,
> 
> Hope you’re well — quick intro, I run partnerships at Startup Slate...


From: Maya Cheng <maya@startup-slate.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 20 Dec 2026 14:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Startup Slate — one-off credit carveout and onboarding pricing question

Thanks Chloe — and thanks for looping Ben and Laura. The suggested structure sounds promising and is much closer to what we need. A couple of clarifying questions from our side before you finalize wording:

- Can the 100k token-equivalent credits be front-loaded (first 30 days at full allotment) so teams can demo prototypes during cohort week?
- For the convertibility: we’d prefer the converted discount to apply as a % off the first month’s Dedicated invoice (not restricted to a single "month" bucket), and a 90-day conversion window sounds fine. Are overdrafts / over-usage billing handled the same way during the conversion?
- Invoicing / tax: our finance team wants a clear path — can Redwood issue a single consolidated invoice to Startup Slate / sponsor for any cohort-wide purchases, or does each startup need its own billing?

We’re looking to promote the benefit publicly on Mon morning; an interim PDF with the high-level offer is all we need now. If you can do that, we’ll take it to the partners for approval and follow up with any final legal redlines.

Best,
Maya

On Sun, Dec 20, 2026 at 09:10 AM Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Ben / Laura — looping you in. Maya is asking for a cohort-level carveout...


From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.ai>
To: Maya Cheng <maya@startup-slate.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 21 Dec 2026 11:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Startup Slate — one-off credit carveout and onboarding pricing question

Hi Maya — appreciate the quick back-and-forth and the clear requirements. Per internal review with Finance and Sales, we can provision an interim, candidate offer for your partners and the cohort. See the summary below — I’ve attached a short offer PDF for your materials and a DocuSign link for the simple addendum if you want to fast-track legal.

Candidate offer (pilot):
- Cohort allocation: 12 teams, 100,000 token-equivalent credits per team, active for 90 days from issuance. Credits are non-transferable between accounts but assigned at account level for each participating startup.
- Front-loading: first 30 days will not be throttled — teams get immediate access to their full allotment to support demo week.
- Convertibility: any unused credit balance may be converted at the cohort-account owner’s request into a one-time 20% off the first month invoice of a Dedicated commitment. The Dedicated commitment must be a minimum 3-month purchase and conversion must be requested within 90 days of credit issuance.
- Marketplace stacking: cohorts may combine the credit allocation with marketplace reserved pricing; however, convertibility is limited to one applied discount per account to avoid stacking multiple committed discounts.
- Billing: we can support consolidated sponsorship billing (Startup Slate or sponsor invoices Redwood for the cohort allocation) OR individual invoicing per startup — we’ll need the sponsor/legal/finance details to determine tax handling and invoicing party.

Next steps:
1) If this works, sign the attached addendum (DocuSign link included) and provide the list of participating accounts by Jan 5 so we can provision credits on Jan 8.
2) Ben will follow up with a short COGS/impact note and Laura will confirm invoicing flows for sponsor vs individual billing.

Attachments: Startup_Slate_Offer_20261221.pdf
DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/embedded/sign/abc123-startup-slate-offer
HubSpot: https://hubspot.example/contacts/4982

Happy to hop on a quick 15-min call if your partners want to talk through the mechanics.

Thanks,
Chloe Martin
Director, Partner Programs
Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwood.ai

> Thanks Chloe — and thanks for looping Ben and Laura...

preview:
From: Morgan Ellis <morgan@clearstack.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha_kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-12-20T10:12:03-08:00
Subject: Interest from pricing page — looking for demo & pricing

title: HaloEdge private offer: AWS evaluation timetable & billing route
preview:
From: Sana Iqbal <sana.iqbal@haloedge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Amit Rao <amit.rao@haloedge.com>
Date: Mon, 14 Dec 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Interest in private offer + MAP for AWS procurement

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week — the team liked the latency and the cost projections you showed. Procurement and our platform team have asked me to get the evaluation / procurement plan documented so we can proceed through AWS Marketplace private offer.

High-level asks from our side:
- A mutual action plan (MAP) with milestones and owners for evaluation, legal review, and PO issuance
- A clear billing path for Marketplace private offer (how invoices / billing flow to our AP team)
- Any required security artifacts (SOC2 summary, data handling statements) to accelerate legal review

Can you send a draft MAP and an example private-offer quote that includes the billing path and committed SKUs? We're targeting a procurement kickoff the week of Dec 21 and ideally a signed PO by end of January.

Appreciate it — happy to set a short call to walk through the MAP.

Best,
Sana Iqbal
Head of Platform Operations
HaloEdge



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference.com>
To: Sana Iqbal <sana.iqbal@haloedge.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.inference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Tue, 15 Dec 2026 11:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Interest in private offer + MAP for AWS procurement

Hi Sana — thanks, great to hear HaloEdge wants to move forward. I attached a first-pass MAP and an example AWS private-offer quote (pdf) that maps to the committed capacity you described during the demo. Quick summary below — I tried to align milestones with your Dec 21 kickoff window.

Attached: MAP-draft-HaloEdge.xlsx; aws-private-offer-quote.pdf; redwood-soc2-summary.pdf

Proposed MAP (summary):
- Week 1 (Dec 21): Kickoff + technical onboarding. Owners: HaloEdge (Sana/Amit), Redwood (Ben + Rishi).
- Week 2: Evaluation tests run (latency/cost/perf) against a 7-day workload; Redwood provides short eval guide (attached).
- Week 3: Legal & Security review. Redwood supplies SOC2 summary & data flow doc.
- Week 4: Finalize private offer pricing and billing path; Redwood prepares DocuSign package for Marketplace private-offer acceptance.
- Target PO/Commit: Jan 31, 2027 — finance approvals & PO issuance (HaloEdge AP).

Billing path notes:
- AWS Private Offer will be set up through the Marketplace listing; invoices generated by AWS will list HaloEdge and link to the private offer. Redwood will provide the signed private-offer acceptance; AWS issues invoices to HaloEdge AP according to Marketplace billing cadence.
- If HaloEdge requires vendor-billed invoices (direct Redwood invoices instead of Marketplace invoices), we can discuss a hybrid route but it may delay close because marketplace POs are simplest for your procurement.

Open items I flagged in the MAP:
- KMS/HSM requirement (if HaloEdge wants customer-managed keys for encryption at rest)
- Any additional proof-of-security beyond SOC2 (e.g., penetration test report)
- Whether purchase order needs to reference a specific marketplace listing ID or a partner-managed SKU

Next steps: please review the MAP and mark any items that need adjustment. I'm available for a 30-min call Wed or Thu (Dec 16/17) to walk the plan through your procurement and tech leads.

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.inference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 16 Dec 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Interest in private offer + MAP for AWS procurement

Ben — quick internal note after reviewing the MAP draft. A couple of pricing/ops checkpoints we should confirm before sending the next version to HaloEdge: 

- Confirm committed-tier thresholds in the quote (we included a 3-tier commit model but HaloEdge signaled they may want a small pilot, so we should show pilot pricing + conversion to commit).
- Validate billing flow: do we want to propose Marketplace-only invoicing (recommended) or offer an opt-in for Redwood invoicing with a surcharge? Ops would like to keep marketplace invoicing for simplicity unless AP requires otherwise.
- Ask finance for a one-time migration/enablement credit (small) to speed PO approval. If we offer a $5k enablement credit it may help them commit by Jan 31.

I can join the call with Sana if you want. Also, add a bullet in the MAP about transition support (first-month SRE hours included).

Thanks,
Lauren
Director, Commercial Ops
Redwood Inference


From: Sana Iqbal <sana.iqbal@haloedge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Amit Rao <amit.rao@haloedge.com>, procurement@haloedge.com
Date: Fri, 18 Dec 2026 08:47:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Interest in private offer + MAP for AWS procurement

Thanks Ben — this looks useful. A few clarifying points after reviewing with procurement and platform: 

- We would like the MAP to explicitly call out the procurement artifacts our AP team needs: marketplace listing ID, private-offer acceptance form (DocuSign), W-9 (if vendor-billed), and a sample invoice.
- KMS: we prefer bring-your-own-key (customer-managed KMS) for any data at rest in Redwood-hosted buckets. Please flag any technical constraints.
- Pilot: procurement supports a 30-day pilot with a capped spend and conversion terms; they want the MAP to show pilot -> committed-tier conversion mechanics.

If you can send an updated MAP that includes those artifacts and a sample DocuSign link for the private-offer acceptance, we'll route it to procurement this coming Monday (Dec 21) and request a kickoff. Also send the SOC2 summary (we didn't see the pen test but SOC2 will be helpful for legal).

On timings: target dates look good. Please include an explicit PO issuance target in the MAP (we will aim for Jan 31 but procurement may require signoff cycles).

Best,
Sana
Head of Platform Operations
HaloEdge


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference.com>
To: Sana Iqbal <sana.iqbal@haloedge.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.inference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.inference.com>, procurement@haloedge.com
Date: Mon, 20 Dec 2026 16:49:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Interest in private offer + MAP for AWS procurement

Sana — thanks for the clear feedback. Attached is v2 of the MAP (MAP-draft-HaloEdge.xlsx) which: 
- Adds a procurement checklist (marketplace listing ID, sample invoice, DocuSign private-offer acceptance, W-9)
- Shows pilot -> committed-tier conversion rules and example math for a 30-day capped pilot
- Calls out KMS/BYOK option and the small extra step needed for key rotation integration (we can schedule an SRE session during Week 1 to scope).

Attachments: MAP-draft-HaloEdge.xlsx; HaloEdge-eval-guidelines.docx; redwood-soc2-summary.pdf

DocuSign / Next-step link: https://www.docusign.com/en/link/EXAMPLE-DS-12345
HubSpot record: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/1234567/company/haloedge (deal RDW-PO-2026-0912)

Action items I will take now:
- Loop in finance to confirm whether we can provide a $5k enablement credit and confirm implications for the private-offer quote. (Lauren / Rishi to confirm.)
- Prepare the private-offer acceptance DocuSign package and a sample Marketplace invoice for procurement to review.
- Schedule a 30-min technical kickoff with HaloEdge (Sana/Amit) for Dec 22 or Dec 23 — please advise preferred window.

Sana — if you can confirm a primary AP contact and target PO date (best guess), we'll finalize the DocuSign package and quote. Looking forward to the kickoff.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
Attachments: MAP-draft-HaloEdge.xlsx (spreadsheet), HaloEdge-eval-guidelines.docx (doc), redwood-soc2-summary.pdf (pdf)
